You have to assume that you're better off for lack of a better phrase. Dumming everything down. Simple. Simple is better. And so I already said that when it comes to the hook right what I'm constantly thinking about is what's an extraordinary way of presenting the ordinary. If I'm dealing with an ordinary topic what is an extraordinary way to talk about the ordinary that is kind of the foundation of right. So you've got the ordinary you're talking about the common right don't simply talk about the common because you're going to trigger that mental opt out you're going to trigger trigger that categorical imperative what's the extraordinary way to talk about the the ordinary. And so let's talk a little bit about finding seeds and hooks seeds seeds of hooks. And so finding seeds of ideas come comes from really two things and this is really the simplest way to to to think about it and that is this it comes from input and it comes from connections input meaning the information that you are consuming right the reality is is that I know people talk about you know people there are folks out there that say you know don't watch TV don't watch the news you know you know don't read fiction or you know only read about your topic read about marketing this that the reality is is that the quality of your input is ultimately going to determine the quality of industry the niche but also consuming information not related to to the industry niche or the topic consuming general information and consuming a variety of information and then from that input and that that volume of input and that quality of input then what we do is we look for connections and that's really the the the the foundation of all idea generation like if you want if you want another you need because it it demonstrates that the foundation of of good hooks and ideas are in seeing connections in actively looking for connections between two seemingly disconnected things I always say that two of my smartest friends the people that the marketers that I think are are two of the smartest marketers out there are rich shephard and jay abraham and the one thing that those guys both have in common and they happen to also be be um good friends I met jay through rich one of the things that they have both of those guys have in common is I think they naturally see connections where others don't they naturally see connections where others don't and so they're able to uh to create ideas new ideas by seeing connections that others don't they are they're they are they're able to see they're able to take something that is out in front of people here and something else that's also out in front of people here and while most people see them as you know uh disparate you know like um separate isolated uh ideas or elements or things those guys are able to see the connection between the two and that's where ideas come from all ideas all good ideas are just connections that weren't there before that's the reality right like the bible talks about there's nothing new under the sun the reality is is that they're you know we're not creating anything we're bringing together right we don't create we bring together in a new and different uh combination and so that's why I said that hooks ideas they're developed right like they're they're developed what I mean in this context by developed not discovered is that you're not going to discover a a great hook a great theme a great big marketing idea in its complete form right like you're not going to stumble on something and be like that's the idea you're going to stumble on something that is the seed of an idea and then we're going to work on it we're going to right move it we're going to look at it from a different angle and bring in something else right and so we're going to develop it we're looking for right we're if anything we're discovering the seeds of hooks and ideas through our input through connections and this was really all about was this the the the kind of the the ordinary idea here the ordinary concept the ordinary idea behind this promotion was that um fiber optic cable was you know was spreading across the united like was being the late across the united states and that was going to open up a tremendous opportunity in the telecommunications industry right by the way catalyst right catalyst right fiber optic cable being laid across the you know cable being laid across the united states right opening up an opportunity to make money with the um the telecommunications industry and so um right that's the ordinary idea uh and what porter was able to see is he was able to see a connection between that and between when the railroad was first built and laid across the united states when the railroad was laid across the united states which is what he talks about here it created tremendous generational wealth and so in porter's mind he saw this fiber optic cable being laid across the united states and the impact that it was going to have on the telecommunications industry which would open up a tremendous investing investment opportunity for people he saw a connection between that and the same thing that happened years ago with the railroad and so he combined that right he he brought that connection together and that became the seed of his idea that there's a new railroad across america and it is making people very rich and he and he ties what's happening now to what happened years ago that's a great example to me of connection right that's the lazy marketer and listen to me the lazy marketer the lazy copyrighter what they would have done is they would have just had a promotion that's like how to get wealthy or how to get rich off of the coming telecommunications whether it's a long form sales letter whether it is you know a video sales letter or a webinar even a multi-part video sequence this is how I think about um these campaigns now this is specific to the front end so I'm talking about here front end campaigns campaigns to turn prospects into buyers I'm not talking about back end campaigns and so what we've got is just to break this down is you'll see that there are two sections here the uh this entire timeline I want you to view as your your campaign your your copy your promotion whatever you want to call it 75 percent of it is marketing only 25 percent of it is selling in this context as I mentioned yesterday for me the selling the selling is really the presentation of the offer this is really where I'm introducing the opportunity for the prospect to get the offer to take advantage of the offer I'm introducing the offer I'm telling them everything they get with it what it'll do for them what what they have to give blah blah blah all that kind of stuff so the the where I am talking about the product is in this last 25 percent this 75 percent is as I told you guys yesterday is where I everything that I'm doing saying showing presenting and demonstrating is ultimately there to lead the prospect to that one buying belief so I told you guys yesterday I showed you guys how I I start off by identifying after the offer is created I start off by by identifying what is that one buying belief that I need these prospects to have we covered that yesterday the the campaign thesis the marketing thesis right and the aim of of the um the ultimately the aim of the marketing section of the campaign is to get buy in on that belief and so just like I told you guys yesterday the way that I think about this is I'm a prosecutor you yesterday just to reillustrate this point of how this works is I want you to understand something here and this is an important point you see in this 75 percent in this 75 percent of what it is that I am saying in a front end customer acquisition client acquisition campaign I don't care what the offer is in the end whether it's for a strategy session whether it's for a course whether it's for a book whether it's for a training program with whether it's for a conference I don't care in the 75 percent I am not talking about the product I'm not pitching a product I'm not selling the product I'm not getting buy-in on the product I'm getting buy-in on the method to deliver the result that the prospect wants to alleviate alleviate the pain that they want alleviated to give them the feelings that they want to have in other words this 75 percent is getting buy-in on the mechanism on the method right it's getting buy-in on the mechanism the framework the system the approach the unique mechanism the thing that is different and superior to every other method process system framework formula out there I'm not introducing the product and I'm not pitching or selling the product until I am presenting the offer in this last 25 percent in this last 25 percent and so that's an important point for you to understand because remember the thesis this is why and I don't know if if he delivers the mechanism through the product through the through the service and so you create an all roads lead to you approach you create this all roads lead you because you are the only source of that unique mechanism you're not selling or marketing a commodity you're not positioning your service as a as a commoditized thing that they can get somewhere else you're positioning it as proprietary it's only available through you that's the importance of the unique mechanism the importance of the unique mechanism part of it is that it prevents the google slap what I call the google slap trigger where somebody says somebody here's what you're talking about they press pause on your video they go over to google and they do a search they go over to amazon and they do a search they go over to ebay they do a search they go over to youtube and they do a search and then they find other elements see we're never ever presenting your solution as a as a commodity we're never allowing or you to listen to me I want you to pay attention to this nuance okay that the in that campaign thesis you'll notice that that the the unique mechanism is one of the three pieces right there's the statement of superiority there's the primary promise and there is the unique mechanism if you took the unique mechanism out right and you put in something like a commoditized solution or just an this up let's do this so since ed brought this up i might as well go through this very very quickly this is shared in depth in um this is kind of a strategic thing but so cpb uh chunks are the way that the the way that the argument is put together the way that i want you to think about this is cpb one let's do this plus cpb um two uh plus cpb three let's just say plus cpb um four equals buying belief meaning right so we let's go back to for for a second let's go back to the um um let's go back to the what was i going to say the prosecutor so yesterday i said to you guys so the prosecutor knows that he or she wants to uh wants to lead the jury to the to the conclusion that the defendant is guilty right and should be punished to the full extent of the law so the prosecutor knows that in order to lead the jury to that belief the prosecutor needs to establish other sub beliefs along the way like that the um the the dude was um had a motive right like the the guy had a motive maybe the next thing that the the guy had a violent tendency um the next thing that the the the prosecutor needs the jury to believe is that he was seen at the um at the the the the he was at the scene of the crime let's just say and let's just say hypothetically let's do this and let's say hypothetically that is all that the prosecutor needed to um needed to do so in that case there would be three cpb chunks right i'm giving you an ultra fast overview of this but three cpb chunks right each cpb chunk the way to look at it is each chunk each chunk takes care of one belief the the the prospect needs needs to lead them to the buying belief okay each each chunk each cpb chunk um establishes one belief that is necessary to lead the prospect to the buying belief so in this example we said that the first um the first belief that we need the prospect that we need the jury to have is we need the jury to believe that uh the defendant had a motive right and so what are we gonna do we are simply going to take that belief that we need the jury to have and we're gonna claim it as true that's it we're gonna take the belief that we need the jury to have and we are gonna claim it as true so we're gonna say the uh defendant um the defendant had a motive. That becomes the claim. Then we're going to offer proof for that. This becomes, so CPB, by the way, CPB stands for claim proof benefit. And so right, the defendant had a, had a motive, right? Let's just say according to their, you know, their financials, they were in, I don't know, foreclosure on their home and their cars were being, you know, whatever, repoed. And, and right, according, according to the front. And let's say I'm just using this as a totally made up example. And here, and here are several emails showing they were ready to take extreme measures to fix their finances and hears them on the life insurance policy, right? Like that's policy, right? Boom. Okay. Right. Let's just say, let's just imagine that was enough. So Dean, that's all covered in E5 coaching. And then, right, so that's what the, that's what the prosecutor would do, right? And so the defendant had a motive. Next, what was the next belief? The next, the defendant has a violent tendency, right? What's the proof of that? You know, he's been arrested six times in the past, you know, 10 months. He's committed blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, right? We're going to, we're going to give evidence of that. Next claim is that the defendant was, you know, seen at the, was at the scene of the crime, the defendant was at the scene of the crime, right? Proof. Here's the video from, you know, across the street or whatnot. And Bob, the owner said he saw him, let's hear from Bob or whatever, he saw him there, right? This is basically the format that a prosecutor would, would use, right? Like the defendant had a motive. Here's the proof, according to their financials, boom, the defendant of violent history has been arrested six times in the past 10 months. He's committed these crimes. The defendant was at the scene of the crime. Here's the video from across the street and Bob, the owner said, right, Bob, I saw him there. That's the format. We use the same format, except we add one other element and that is the benefit. So for example, we might, we might want the prospect to believe that, that, you know, claim this is, this works in a matter of, you know, this, let me keep this real simple. This is here. Anybody can do this. It's super simple. What is the proof of that? The proof of that, it only requires three, three clicks of the mouse and works on any computer, any computer, any computer, right? So the benefit for you, no, you regardless of your experience, you can do this and be seeing, you know, savings in a matter of days, something like that, right? This becomes a, this is an example of a CPB chunk. So all we're doing, so to go back to Edward, all we are doing is we are taking the things that they need to believe, to believe the buying belief, right? To lead them to the buying belief, right? You get, you lead prospects to the buying belief, right? Through these sub beliefs and you create these sub beliefs in their mind by claiming them to be true, offering proof, support, evidence and then take, giving them the takeaway, the benefit of that. Anybody can do this. It's super simple. It only requires three clicks of the mouse, works on any computer. So regardless of your experience, you can do this and be seeing savings in a matter of days. See how simple that is in terms of a chunk? That is the, that's the make up of the marketing argument. It literally becomes this, it becomes, right? It's almost like this. It's hook, right? To get attention, right? Lead to create engagement, set the emotional hook, right? Why you should listen to me and this is quick. That's it. And then we go into, right? The call it marketing argument that is made up of, let's do this and say it's this and then it is, oops, segue into offer, offer and then it becomes this. That's the strategic overview. So to answer that question, there's the answer to that question for you, Ed. And so let's now go back to this. And so I want to talk a little bit about presenting proof. You saw in that, right? You saw in the Bar of Best Seller copy that there was proof upon proof upon proof upon proof. I'll tell you that, right? No proof, no marketing campaign, no proof. You simply have a bunch of claims, right? Like, and the reality is, is that, is that, right? Is that anybody today can make claims. Anybody can claim anything to be true about anything. What turns it from a bunch of claims where you're simply just expecting prospects to believe you. See, that's the thing that I want you to understand, that what a lot of marketers do is they simply expect their prospects to believe them because they said it. But the reality is the healthiest perspective for you to take is that your prospects will not believe you unless you offer evidence support, right? They're going to say what they're going to say. You've heard this, right? Like those of you that have studied copy, you've heard like, yeah, right. Sure. How do I know that that's true? Just because you're saying it doesn't mean it's true. People say a lot of outlandish things online. We see that every day, right? There are people on here right now that have said outlandish things. And so, and so the, the, the reality is, is that what turns it from a string of claims into a, an argument is the proof. It's the proof, right? The claim, right? Anybody can make, right? Anybody can make a string of claims. This is great. This is fantastic. This works easy. This is super. This is good. That's what the typical marketer does. That's why it feels like it feels like marketing. It feels like a sales pitch and it feels like, you know, puffery. Like how do I know you're just making a bunch of claims? It sounds great, but how do I know that that's true? Where is the logical support? Where is the logical support? Right? And so the, the, the point is, right? That adding in the proof, the evidence, the support, I'm going to show you the simplest way to do this is, is where it becomes an argument. Now, when you add in the benefit, the takeaway for the prospect, then it becomes a marketing argument. So when you have a claim and then the proof and then the benefit to the prospect, what that means for you is, so what you can do is, so you'll enjoy, so you'll see, so you'll experience, so you'll get, now it becomes a marketing argument. And when done right, it leads the prospect to the buying belief, which perfectly sets up the sale. And so you need proof. And so you must give support. You must give proof for, you must give evidence to back up whatever you claim. Do not go into your marketing, marketing campaigns, assuming that your market will believe you simply because you say it. That's what the novice does. That's what the lazy does. Right? And yeah, you will get some people, you know, you will get some people to just take your word for it, to take a leap of faith. But if you want to put up big numbers, serious numbers, you want to reinforce that emotional desire with the logical support, with the evidence, right? With the evidence, right? With the evidence. And so, and that's also what turns it from a bunch of claims into an argument, what turns it from a bunch of claims, a pitch, if you will, into an educational presentation. Because you're giving them the evidence, you're giving them the logical ah-has. You're not just claiming it's great, claiming it's easy, claiming it's fast, claiming it's simple, claiming it works, claiming it's superior. Anybody can say mine is the best, right? The question becomes why? What makes it the best? How does it work that makes it more effective? How does it work that makes it quicker? How, why does it put up quicker results than anything else? Convince me of that, right? While you'll get some people that will buy when you don't offer that, if you want the masses to buy, you need that support. And so, you have to give support, evidence proof, for whatever it is that you claim. Don't claim what you can't prove. Don't claim what you cannot prove, right? If you can't prove why it, it works quickly, don't claim it. Don't claim it, because what I want you to understand, you are better off and listen to me. In your copy, you are better off taking out unsubstantiated claims, even though they're exciting, right? Like they're exciting, they're benefit-driven, they're blah, blah, blah, blah. You're better off taking out unsubstantiated claims than you are leaving them in there unsubstantiated, because those unsubstantiated claims, the more that you make, they stack together to create this big feel of disbelief, right? Of like, you're stretching it, like, you're asking me, right? It's almost like you make an unsubstantiated claim. I take a step back, right? Like, or really, I should say a step closer to the cliff, like, all right, so I'm going to have to take his word on this. This is her word on this. This is a bit of a leap of faith. You make another unsubstantiated claim, well, now there's more of a leap of faith. You make another unsubstantiated claim, now there's more of a leap of faith. And eventually, when you have all of these unsubstantiated claims, now it becomes, my decision is based on a gigantic leap of faith. And so you are better off taking out those unsubstantiated claims, right? Like, don't make it. Don't claim what you can't prove. And no claim should be bigger than your biggest proof point. In other words, like, in practicality, right, in practical terms, if you are, let's say, you have evident, let's say hypothetically, you have an example, a case study of how this technique was used, and it produced results in three days, right? And, right, most people get results in seven days, some five days, some eight days, some 10 days, and you've got an example of somebody that used this and got results in three days. Well, don't claim that you can see results in 24 hours, because you have no way to prove that. Your biggest proof point in that small example is of three days. Therefore, your biggest claim should be no more than or less than three days, right? So your, your, your no claim should be bigger than your biggest proof point. Don't do not claim what you cannot prove. Now, when it comes to proof, support and evidence, and I use these, those, those words interchangeably, and I use those words interchangeably for a reason, because I want you to look at, right? I said every claim must have support, evidence, proof, right? Because I want you to understand that there is like that most people, when they think of proof, they think of testimonials, they think of stats, they think of, you know, studies, they think of, you know, quotes from people, you know, most marketers, they immediately default to testimonials case studies, testimonials case studies, most like, like a lot of, of clients even that come into the E5 coaching are always like, I'm beat then because I don't have any client testimonials, right? They're assuming that client testimonials are the only type of proof point or the only way to give evidence or support for their claims. And they're wrong because they don't understand that there are different types of proof points to be used at different instances in your marketing campaign. So the first type of proof that, that, that everybody is familiar with is social proof, right? This is where I would, I would really put the, this is really what I would call it. I almost, I almost labeled it as this on here, but I would call this, you know, product proof, right? This is proof evidence that your product will deliver what it is that you say it will. And the best proof to use in, in, in that, in this case is testimonials, client testimonials, client case studies, client results, that sort of stuff, right? And so client testimonials, customer testimonials, customer results, those are great to use as proof points during your offer. And by the way, just as a side note, as a side note, what I'll say is the best way to use testimonials is strategically as a proof point to reinforce a claim. And so what I mean by that is if you claim that, you know, that, that you're giving some deliverable, you're giving a fill in the blank roadmap, which makes it brain dead simple to use. Therefore, you could be up and running in 15 minutes with this thing. Well, the best testimonials to use right after as a proof point for that would be some sort of testimonial or case study of a client that used it and got results right away or was up and running in 15 minutes, right? Rather than a testimonial that says, you know, I love the system, it changed my whole life. That's a great testimonial, but that's not the best testimonial to use strategically at that point, because you just made a claim about how quickly this one thing is going to help you get started. The best thing to do is to use a testimonial that talks about how simple and quick it is to get started, right? And so you've got to recognize that testimonials are a proof point. Well, what are we trying to prove? We're trying to prove claims that we're making. We make claims and then we offer proof. The right way or the best way, the most impactful way to use a client testimonial is to use it to offer proof for a claim that we made about the product and what the product can do, deliver, blah, blah, blah, the experience with the product and so on, right? This is why as a side note, right, as crazy as this may sound, we've got campaigns where I've used a testimonial, if you will, about the guarantee, right? How somebody took advantage of the guarantee. Like if we say you'll be able to, you'll be able to, you know, one simple email and you'll be able to get all your money back, no hassle, no problems, same day, right? Well, some people question that, like, yeah, right, how do I know that that's true? Maybe you're just saying that and then you're not going to give me my money back and you're going to screw me, right? And so put a testimonial there, somebody that says, hey, I realized this was a little premature for my purchase. I sent that one email and I did get my money back. I was astonished. I love this company. I'll definitely be doing business with them again in the future. That's great proof point. It proves that what we're claiming in the guarantee. But you see how that wouldn't be a good testimonial to use when I'm talking about the speed of which it produces results, right? It doesn't make any sense because it's not proving that. It's proving something else. And so you want to use those testimonials strategically in the right place to prove the claims that you are making. Now, this is the one type of proof point, but there is also what I call claim proof, claim proof. And this is proof to support the claims that we're making about the mechanism that does not require you to have customer or client testimonials. You don't need client testimonials or client case studies to prove the claims that you're making about the mechanism. You do not need client testimonials to back up claims that you're making about the mechanism. And so let me show you what I call, right? So this is covers what we just said about the strategic use of client testimonials. Let's talk about presenting claim proof. And so here is the simplest way for you guys to think about this. That member I said yesterday and I want you to listen to me, okay? We're coming down. We're coming to the home stretch in terms of we've got 30 more minutes and then we're going to break for lunch. I'm going to finish up here, answer the questions, send you off to lunch and then we're going to get back to after lunch, nitty gritty details, copy like blah, blah, blah. I said the single most important thing that you can do with your unique mechanism. What did I say? What did I say yesterday? Somebody pop it into the chat. What's the single most valuable thing, most important thing you could do when it comes to crafting engineering your unique mechanism? Was it naming the unique mechanism? Was that it? What was it? Make it feel different, okay? What else? Make it feel superior, okay? Interrogating. Okay. So what I said was, right, you, so for Ali, it, right, a unique mechanism by nature. If it's a unique mechanism, then it's different, right? And so absolutely, we've got a different way, right? Like that's critical, right? If it's not a different way, then it's not a unique mechanism. It's just a mechanism, right? We don't want to sell mechanisms because mechanisms are commodities. We want to sell unique mechanisms. That's why also as a side note, right? That's why we have to do the examination because we need to know what competitors are saying about their mechanisms so that we don't model them or copy them. If we copy them, we no longer have a unique mechanism. We just have a mechanism. If our mechanism is available elsewhere, it's not a unique mechanism. It's a mechanism. No, that's a commodity. We're not selling commodities. We're selling proprietary solutions. Of course, like Jan, you were spot on, our mechanism needs to be viewed or we need to lead the prospect to see that it is superior. It's not enough to be different. Different gets attention. Superior generates the sale. Different gets attention. Superior generates the sale. That's why we use different and superiors. Different gets attention. Superior to everything else. That's what produces the sale. But the key is, right? And we talked about Shara, right? Like that's the fastest, more efficient, right? That's claiming superiority. It's got it. You've got to have something in there. But you've got to be clear, of course, Martin, about how it works differently, right? And so you be clear about how it works differently goes back to what Dan said, which is interrogating. The single most valuable thing that you can do is spend time understanding your method, your process, your framework. That's right. It's the interrogating, asking yourself the questions of why do I do it that way? Why do I do what I do? Why do I do it in that order? What would happen if I change the order? What would happen if I took away a step? What would happen if I added in a step? What makes these steps better than the other seven steps that people do? Spending time understanding the mechanism is the most valuable thing that you can do. Number one, I'll tell you this, and I want you to listen to me. I want you to listen to me carefully here. Let me do this. I want to show you something. So let's go to this. So remember, hang with me, right? Another 30 minutes, and then you guys are on lunch break, and you can decompress. Hang with me. This is really important. And so I said to you guys that everything that we do here, right? Everything that we're doing here in the marketing argument is there to lead the prospect to the buying belief, right? We're leading the prospect to accept the fact that and to realize that the best way for them to experience the promise, the outcome, the result, the transformation, the alleviation of pain, the alleviation of their symptoms, and to feel the way that they want to feel is with this unique mechanism. And so we're going to prove to them why that is the case. Well, what I want you to understand is when a marketer thinks that they understand the principle, the concept, the idea, the tactic, the strategy of the unique mechanism, often what they do is they think that the power in the unique mechanism is in the name. They think that, right? They think meaning that, well, I do three steps to help people. I teach people three steps for Facebook ads. Let me call it, let me see. What can I call it? Let me call it the palm tree process. I'm going to call it the palm tree process. And I'm going to say to folks that this is different and superior. My palm tree process of setting up Facebook ads is different and it's superior to every other method out there. And they use that and they get attention and they do, right? And then they have a lead and they talk about all the wonderful things that they unpack and tease all the wonderful things with the palm tree process for creating from driving traffic with Facebook, right? But then what did I say? At the end of the lead, the prospect says now prove to me, right? Prove to me that it works and prove to me that it will work for me. And so in the marketing argument, then we're going to prove, we're going to present, demonstrate, show that the, that that unique mechanism is the superior solution, right? That it is better than all of the others. And so now for that marketer that just took a, took their three steps, their, their, you know, their three steps and they slapped the fancy pants name on it and they called it the palm tree process. Now, when it comes time to prove that it is superior, what do they say? What, what do they present? How do you prove that it is, how do you prove that it's superior? How do you prove that it is, it is better? How do you prove that it is better when you just took the ordinary and you slapped a unique name on it? They don't have a unique mechanism. They have a unique name for a commodity. That's all they, they did. They have a unique name for a commodity. They took something common without diving into it, without understanding the nuance of it, without understanding why they do it, right? Without interrogating it, they took a commodity and they slapped a unique name on it. And now when it comes time to the marketing argument for them to prove what makes it superior, how do you prove that a commodity with a unique name is superior? You can't because you're starting with a commodity. That's why I said to you that the power is not in the name. The name is valuable and the name is important, right? But I said the name is just about credibility. It's just, it should sound scientific, right? At the end of the day, the single most valuable thing is to understand how and why your method, your process, your framework works. The reason why is because it gives you the ability to offer evidence and support. Let me show you how. Notice in what it is that I just said, I said the reason why is this phrase and this phrase are two of the most valuable phrases that you could use in copy when you want to offer proof. Gary Benzavango, one of the greatest copywriters ever said that the word because is one of the most powerful words there is. And the reason why is no pun intended because it serves as a proof point, as evidence, as support. Meaning I can make a claim and then if I say the reason why is or I make a claim and then I say because, well, what I say after that serves as evidence. It is now viewed as a substantiated claim. And so meaning that if you look at this, so this ensures that your ad account never gets banned. What is that? That's a claim, right? That's a claim. If I said, right, this ensures that your ad account never gets banned. And that's all I do. And I leave it there. That's just a claim. As a prospect, I could say, well, how do I know that that's true? Of course, like you're so you're saying your ad account never gets banned. How's that? Why is that? Why should I believe that? Like, why is that? Give me the logical support. Give me the logical support so that I can accept this claim. Give me something to hang it on rather than asking me to take a leap of faith. Right. And now I say this ensures that your ad account never gets banned because and now what comes next serves as evidence. It leverages only compliant copy. In other words, what we're saying is the reason why your account will never get banned is because it uses this compliant copy. The compliant copy is the reason why your ad account would never get banned. And see, so what what comes after the word because or the phrase the reason why serves as evidence, it serves as support. So over here, your grass will grow rapidly. What is that? That's a claim. That's a claim. That's all it is. If I just said your grass will grow rapidly, your grass will be green, your grass will be it'll be it'll be thick. Your your your neighbors will be astonished. Right. It works right away. It's easy to use. It's so so all that is is just a string of claims. Anybody can put together a string of claims. The it becomes a marketing argument when we give the support the evidence and the easiest way to give that is the rationale. Your grass will grow rapidly. The reason why is because of the fast uptake of nutrients which trigger growth chemicals in the seed. Right. Your grass will grow rapidly. The reason why is because right. It gives the prospect the ability to say, Oh, okay, make sense. Might not understand the nitty gritty details of how that fast uptake works and what that and what that means and the chemicals but right like right. And so right like it gives us the logical support to say, Okay, that makes sense. Right. That makes sense. And so we don't like look, you certainly can use if there was a scientific study of Harvard tested this thing and showed that over a double blind placebo or whatever, blah, blah, blah, this lawn grew faster than, you know, cool. That's a great proof point that you can use, but you don't need that. Like it doesn't have to be that when in doubt, give the rational support, give the reasons why. Now the thing to understand is this, where do those reasons why come from? Like in this, it leverages only compliant copy. Where does that come from? Or over here, the reason why is because the fast uptake of nutrients which trigger growth chemicals in the seed, where does that come from? It comes from the interrogating of the method. You see, that's the beauty. The beauty is that when you interrogate your method, when you understand why it works, like, why is the like, I could take something like the big marketing idea. And I could say, well, why does that work to get attention so well? What's the reason why that works to get attention so well? What's the reason why that's able to cut through the noise compared to typical hooks? What are typical hooks doing like typical marketers, entrepreneurs, you know, novice copywriters, what are they doing that's not being done in the big idea? What is a big idea doing that they're not doing? Why does the big idea work? What does it trigger psychologically? What is it trigger mentally? Is it tapping into both emotion and logic? Is it it's also compliant, right? And so see through the interrogating of that, I'm able to identify, okay, so this gets better, this gets more attention, a big marketing idea than anything else, right? You'll get more attention with a big idea than any other type of hook a claim. The reason why is now I uncovered that during the interrogation. I uncovered that during the, when I asked why, why do I use the big marketing idea? Right? Why do I teach the big marketing idea? Why do I rely on the big marketing idea? But if I said the single best way to differentiate, right? Like, there's no more powerful way to differentiate than with a unique mechanism. Well, right? And the reason why is now where is that going to come from? The reason why is, well, what is the reason why? What's the reason why? Because, right? Why do I use and teach the unique mechanism? Because I believe that it is the most powerful way to differentiate. If I didn't believe it was the most powerful way, just like you with your stuff, I would teach something else. I would use something else. But I do believe it's the most powerful. Why? Now I've got to interrogate, right? Now I've got to interrogate why it is the most powerful. And the beauty is that what that interrogation does is it equips you to give the support. It equips you to give the reason why. And so the reality is you can have an entire marketing argument that simply has that reason why proof. It's easy. The reason why is that it works quickly. And that's because that anybody can do this. And that's because, right? Like you could see results in as small as, you know, in as little as but two hours. Thanks to, right? And like all of that is just reason why it's just evidence is just support. And that evidence and support comes from interrogating the method. And so you've got to realize that you don't need, you don't need, you don't need the like client testimonials, case studies, you don't need stats, you don't need charts, you don't need those things. Those things are valuable. Yes, there's like 11 different types of proof points that you can use. This is the easiest. And this is wildly effective. And this is my default for the majority of claims that I make. And so you could think about a claim, you give support for that claim, and then you give the benefit, right? Like this ensures that your ad account never gets banned, right? Because it only leveraged, it leverages only compliant copy. And so what that means for you is, right? And so what that means for you is you, right? Like you never have to worry about logging into your Facebook account and seeing that horrific notification that your ad account has been banned, you could sleep comfortable at night knowing that your biggest source of traffic is safe. Next, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. Now, I want to do, whoops, I want to do one more thing here. And I want to show you, you know, the some claims that you make, we talked a little bit about this yesterday, some claims that you make are way outside the realm of believability to your prospect. They might stretch their, you know, their their belief, it might become, you know, incredulous, like it becomes, it's too much of a stretch. Well, so you might use more than one source of proof, right? Like in other words, right, some claims can be proven, you know, you can have one proof point, some claims might need two, some big bold claims might be three, three is a unique number, three is an interesting number when it comes to anything, but when it comes to proof in particular, because one time, like one piece of evidence, right, like is an event and it could be a fluke, right? Two, could be considered a coincidence. Three is a pattern. And that's the way the mind processes it, processes it. One is an event, two is a maybe a coincidence, maybe, you know, like, right, like, three is a pattern, right? Three is a pattern. And so, right, if you had a, let's say a trading technique, right, and you invested in a, you use this trading technique and you had a winner, and somebody was like, I use this trading technique once and it produced a winner, you'd be like, all right, maybe it was a fluke, right? I use this trading thing once, winner, I used it again the following week, winner, all right, maybe, you know, maybe you got lucky, maybe it's a coincidence, I used it, right, winner, I used it the following week, winner, I used it this week, another winner, now you've got a pattern and meaning that it is more believable, right? It now goes from this, this, maybe it was a coincidence, maybe it was a fluke to pattern. And so, for big, bold claims, one of the things that I learned from Mark Ford, right, and this is just a, like, just a way, like, something for you to kind of process, file away in your, in your mind, right, that when you make a big, bold claim, one that stretches their believability, one that risks them saying, I'm out, no way, no freaking way, right, like, when we said, you know, if we made a big, ridiculous claim, and you have to be aware too, by the way, as a side note, you have to be aware of, you have to know your market to know enough about what claims are going to be outside, like, what claims are they going to be like, you know, I need some proof for that, and what claims are they going to be like, no freaking way, right, like, no way, that requires you knowing the market, right, like, if I said something like, you know, you know, opt-in pages are starting to die, or something like that, well, that's not for the market as a whole, that's not a claim that is too far outside the realm of possibility, right, like, that is believable, I might want to see some support for that, right, but that is believable, it's not outside the realm of possibility, but if I said, if I made a claim that, you know, online funnels are no longer working, or online funnels are dead in the water, let's say, something along those lines, well, that's a big, bold, audacious claim that the majority of the market is going to be like, no way, right, and that comes from knowing the market, so when, you know, the value of knowing the market is, it allows you to understand how far away from where the prospect is, is this claim, like, is it within the realm of believability and support will, right, or is it way, like, is it stretching them way too much for these big, bold claims, like, these ones that are really, really stretching them, exposing you to the risk of them bailing, this is where you can offer these three consecutive paragraphs of proof, and ideally, right, this is the way that I learned it from Mark, you put the strongest proof at the top, and as Mark said, devote the most ink to it, so you devote the most words to it, the next paragraph should be about half the length, you know, explanation, unpacking of the first, and the third should be half the length of the second, so you lead with your strongest proof point up front, right, you allocate the most amount of copy to it, then you give another proof point, that copy, you allocate half of the copy, roughly, right, this is rough, rough, right, and then you give your weakest proof point last, and that is half the copy of the second one, and ideally, with these three, you vary the proof point, right, and so you vary the type of proof provided. Here's a made-up example from Mark, he said, right, in a test conducted by Byertrends last year, Skippy was rated at, he's talking, this is a mock thing about peanut butter, Skippy was rated as the top-scoring peanut butter in every single category, aroma, appearance, texture, and taste, when compared to other popular brands such as Jiff and Peter Pan, it rated between two and five points higher in each of the categories, Jeff Goodman reporting of Byertrends said, when it comes to the pleasure of eating Skippy, of eating Skippy rules, proof point right here, one proof point right here, second proof point, a scientific analysis conducted by WebNutrition found that Skippy had the highest ratio of protein if out of all the major brands, Patrick Gunn, President of the National Health Institute said Skippy was far and away the best product from a health point of view, second proof point, you could see, and then third, I used to be a Jiff fan myself, but last Thursday for the first time, tried Skippy on a slice of whole wheat toast, and I was blown away by how good it is, point is, right, you're seeing this, like, you see how, right, one is, right, one might not be enough, two, okay, right, three, you got something more powerful, you got to be aware of the claims that you're making and how far away from your prospects they are, they are from the, the claim. The last thing that I want to, that I want to share before we, before I go to the questions and then before we go to lunch is this, that proof is so valuable in copy that when you're doing your research examination, when you're reading, when you're consuming information, when you're putting, when you're, when you're, when you've got those inputs, what I want you to think about is I want you to keep your eyes open for a really strong, really powerful, really big monster proof point, because sometimes you can build an entire hook around a monster proof point. So often what a lot of marketers do, more often than not, I would say 90% of the time is they start with an idea and then they go and they look for proof to back that up. And there's nothing wrong with that, right, that's a valid way of going about it. You have an idea, we need proof that we're going to, you know, to reinforce the, the, the idea, the claims and all that, blah, blah, blah. That's a valid way to do it. But at the same time, you might come across a proof point that is like, wow, that's a monster proof point. That's undeniable. That's like huge, a huge proof point. Can I use that? And is there an idea that I can tie to that proof point that I can use as the hook? And so this is just flipping it on its head, right? This is, this is flipping it on its head. Instead of starting with the idea and looking for proof points, you're starting with the proof point point and looking for ideas, right? Because I would say this, that it's a lot more tedious, it's a lot more tedious work to find a proof point to back up a, a really good idea than it is to find a really good idea to use with a proof point, right? And so in other words, it's easier to develop like, because, ideas are unlimited. The reality is, ideas are infinite out there. Proof points aren't. And so if you come across a monster proof point, right, like that just is undeniable, is extreme, is like crazy, like where you just can't deny it, you make a note of it. And, right? And, and we may be able to develop an idea around that proof point, because we already have got, we already have one of the most critical pieces, which is proving that that, like proving it, right? And so look for monster proof points. And so there you go. So with that, I, what I want to do is I just want to see if, let's see, I want to answer some questions. And then in six minutes, I'll send you off to, to launch. So, so in the context of the chiropractor example, could your unique mechanism be the chiropractor himself, despite selling a commoditized service? No. So I would not do, I would not do that. The chiropractor is not the, is not the unique mechanism. Because remember, the unique mechanism is how does your product or service work to deliver the result? So if the chiropractor learned a bunch of additional modalities, bodywork methods that he or she uses in their practice to help people alleviate their back pain, then that combination of modalities and bodywork methods, that unique combination that they use, that becomes the unique mechanism. In other words, that chiropractor is able to say, right, the reason why I'm able to get the results that I get for people so quickly is because my approach uses a mix of bodywork methods. And here is what they do. And here is why they work. So the chiropractor himself or herself is not the mechanism, what they do and how they do it is the, is the mechanism. And the reality is, is that to dive even deeper on that for one second, that I used to explain many, many moons ago to chiropractors in different languages, but I would explain like, look, unless you do exactly what it is that your competition is doing, every second, every step, every piece with every patient, like from the moment they walk in the door to the moment they leave, right, then you've got a different methodology. Because everything, right, like, do you, you know, what, what, what questions do you ask them on their, on their intake? Why do you ask them those questions? Not insurance and like, why do you ask them those questions? What do you, what type of analysis do you need to do? Do you do any kind of kinesiology? Are you doing muscle testing? Are you doing soft, you know, like a soft connective tissue testing? Are you doing x-rays? Are you doing any other? What are you looking for? Why are you looking for those things? Why are you doing those things? What type of adjustment do you do? Where do you begin? Where do you stop? Why do you do that kind of adjustment? Do you have them do muscle stim after you have them do massage after you have them do any home stretching? Why do you have them do that? Why do you have them do those stretches? All of that together, right, plays a role. It's all of those things in combination that equal the doctor's care and ultimately lead to the results. And so it's that whole thing that becomes that mechanism, that method. The reason why the doctor gets results is not just because of the chiropractic, but it's because it's the chiropractic based on XYZ analysis, followed up with XYZ muscle stim and massage and with home stretches and then cold and heat and this cycle of reducing inflammation. And it's all of that together that he's able to say, right, like, I don't only, right, it's not just chiropractic. It's this method of chiropractic that we use. And it's this method that produces such better results than all the typical chiropractic adjustments that people get. That's the difference. Right. And in that example, when that chiropractor is educating people on his method that he uses of chiropractic combined with inflammation reduction and da da da da, right, what is he doing? He's leading people to see, yeah, that's, that's it. That's the best solution. Not general chiropractic care. He's not selling people on a commodity. He's getting buy in on his method and why his method, why that methodology, that process, that approach, is the way that somebody wants to go creating an all roads lead to him or her. So can the campaign thesis contain more than one buying belief? The best way to start and grow a business is with the XX method. So that's not really more than one buying belief. That is still one buying belief, right, that the best way, the easiest way to start and grow a business, that's still one belief, right? You're not, you're not talking about two different, two different beliefs. And so that still is one belief. And so sure, you can absolutely do that. Awareness, though, even a product aware or fully aware person who already has their wallet out, getting ready to buy a course, a book about funnels will stop. What to do and if I came into the big idea is actually big as in, right. So Michael said, right, even a product aware, fully aware person who already has their wallet out, getting ready to buy a course about book funnels will stop what they're doing and look at the E5 and E5 campaign, if the big idea is actually big as in the barred best seller. Sure, because people are always looking to remember, people are looking to solve problems. And when something grabs their attention is new and different to solve their problem, they want to see if that new and different is better, right? Like this is new, different. This is something I haven't heard before, haven't something I haven't seen before. Is this a better solution for me than what it is that I was considering buying, right? And so that's the power of, of, of different. So Michael is absolutely obviously spot on. So Jan said, Todd, I rather than writing sales pages, I write a lot of ads, but I try to get the hook into the first line of the ad and then establish the unique mechanism of what I'm going to give away, whether it's a resource of VSL, follow the same line you've been explaining. Yeah, I think I understand your question. The answer is yes. So right, you're the hook of your marketing campaign always carries over to your ads. There's got to be congruence between your ad and your campaign. What's one of the biggest mistakes or one of the mistakes that I see folks make with their ads and their landing pages, they've got this. And I see some, this is some, there are some savvy marketers that get their team gets lazy, right? Like I know that their clients and then they build a team and, and then their team runs ads. And what they do is their team runs these ads where they run a variety of different hooks in the ads, right? So a variety of different hooks and they never change the landing page to coincide with the ad. And so what ends up happening is they get a lot of clicks because the hook in the ad caught their attention and then they get to a landing page and the landing page isn't congruent with the hook in the ad and then people bounce. And so as long as the hook, as long as the ad is congruent with the landing page, then you are, you're fine. And yes, the hook always like part of the hook is conveying that you can experience this result with this mechanism, right? Part of the hook is always you can experience this result primary promise with this mechanism. And so Edward said, so for big bold claims, instead of using one, two, three CPB chunk, we should go for one, two, three, one, three, three CPB chunks. You don't want to offer more proof than necessary, but you have to offer enough proof. And so that is like, and that comes from or stems from you understanding what the market knows, what the market believes, right? Like, what do they understand coming in? What's a claim that's going to be a monster stretch for them? And what's a claim that's, that's, that is believable, but they just need to see a proof point, right? So you have to know the market to be able to know like, you got to know who you're talking to, right? To know, you know, like, there are people, right? Like, look, there are people outside of our crazy online marketing world, that if you said to them, right, like, here is how to make money, you know, here's how to make a million, a million dollars or more per month in your sleep, working two hours, they're like, you're crazy, man, like you're nuts. This is absolutely not nuts, crazy, blah, blah, blah. But people in our world, they're like, yeah, I've heard it, I've seen it, you know what I mean? Like, it's, I know that I get it, it's not a stretch. So you have to know your audience to know what claims are going to really stretch them. And so again, we're going to go at rapid pace here. I'm going to go through this, uh, as efficiently and as effectively as I can for you. So we're starting off with a kind of, for lack of a better phrase, a high level view of writing. And I really, and this is kind of, this is the, the approach that you need to take or want to take with your copy. I, you know, this is, I'm talking about here, the formatting, the, the structure. And I, I think of this as simple, short, short. And so first and foremost, before I explain simple, short, short, I want you to understand that copy specifically the, the, you know, the long form sales letters, you know, actual written, written stuff is you want it to sound like the spoken word. You want it to feel like the spoken word. And so you're, you know, there, there is a difference between the written word in a traditional sense and the spoken word, the flow, the, the pace, the choppiness of sentences is different in the spoken word than the written word. You want copy, even in long form sales letter to, um, to feel like the spoken word, not the written word. And so you want to write like you talk. You really want to take the rules of grammar. And you really want to put them, you know, you want to throw them out the window. Nobody's grading your copy in terms of, in terms of whether it's grammatically correct or not. And so you want to make sure that you are, you're communicating the way that you talk. So it should feel like the spoken word. It should feel conversational. Now for what it's worth, you know, I've been asked this question, I think it was, um, I believe it was, uh, the first person to ask me or one of the first people to ask me was, Teddy Garcia on our own team, if, okay, being that it's, it should sound like the spoken word and it, we want to write like we talk or we want the copy to, to, to be more conversational tone. When I'm writing copy, do I dictate it and then, you know, use on the Mac or whatever, use a, a dictation software? The answer is no, I do not. I write it. However, and this has been a key for me personally is that while I'm writing copy, I tend to speak it out loud. So I'm writing it, but I talk through it as I am writing it. I like to write rather than, um, I like to write rather than dictate just because that's the way I started. For whatever reason, my brain is kind of, it just functions better that way. Like I'm able to kind of get the things out of my head, typing, it's almost, it's, I'm able to, it's, it's, I'm able to process as I'm writing, whereas when I'm, when I'm dictating, I tend to go, you know, I tend to be under. And so as you can tell, and so I write, but I talk it out loud and what I'm, I always, I'm writing as I would say it. And if something doesn't feel like I would say it, if, if when I'm, as I'm speaking it, that's not like, that's not how I would say it, it, it doesn't go down on paper. And so what I mean by that is that by speaking it out, I'm speaking it and then, and speaking, typing, speaking, typing, speaking, typing, speaking, typing, speaking, typing. And so, um, and so that's really important, right? You want it to flow. You want it to feel like you're, you know, you want it to feel like you are, are speaking to somebody. And so you want to make sure that, that you write it that way, where you would pause if you were speaking, you want to pause, you want to have a, a, a period, you want to have an ellipsis, you want to, you want to end it, right? If you would say one word and you would emphasize, then you have one word in your copy. And so you want it to feel like, like it is, like it is the spoken word. Next is, right? And some of this, most of this, all of this you guys, you guys get, but I need to drive this home, right? I couldn't do this without driving this home. You want to use simple words. The simplest word possible is ultimately what you want. You don't want to focus on $5 words, $10 words, whatever you want to, whatever you want to call it, big, fancy pants words. You want really the reality is this, you want the smallest number of multi-syllable words as possible. You want to remember, like think of it, carry over the curse of knowledge to copy and recognize that the simpler your copy is, the easier it is to read, the easier it is to understand, the easier it is to consume, the easier it is to make sense of it, to understand what's going on, the easier it is to follow it. And so it all begins with simple words. When there's a simpler, easier word to use, use the simpler word. Don't go over to the thesaurus, you know, to powerthoris, thesaurus.com and start randomly choosing, you know, more elaborate words. Dumb it down. Use the simplest words that you possibly can. It makes it more, it makes it more enjoyable to read, it makes it easier to read, it makes it easier to consume, it makes it easier to understand, it makes it easier to process, and that is a valuable thing. And so simple words. Next is short, choppy sentences. And so one of the best things that you can do, and Ted, I'm going to get to that in just a second. It's also phenomenal for another reason, the Hemingway app, but short, choppy sentences. One of the best ways to see this in action, first of all, is you certainly can read some of Hemingway, like what is it, the old man in the sea, the old man in the sea, I think, what was I going to say, read Lee Child novels. And if you want to subscribe to a newsletter, read the essays written by James Altucher. And what you'll see are very short, punchy, choppy sentences, not these run on lengthy sentences, like really when in doubt, take the comma out, put a period in, and start another sentence. Another great book recommendation, which you can get from my buddy Brian Kurtz, and it really, it's an elaborate way of learning this. But another book by Gene Schwartz, Eugene Schwartz, exactly, Brilliant's Breakthrough. Brilliant's Breakthrough by Gene Schwartz, which you can get from Brian Kurtz, he's now the publisher of that as well, is a great book on on this idea of like subject action period, subject action period, right? Like, you know, he kicked the ball, you know, the ball went in the net, you know, the goal he was pissed, the other team started to celebrate, like period, period, period, period, that believe it or not, right, that short, choppy, punchy, punchy sentences is easier to consume. And it gives the copy momentum. Now, I'm not telling you, right, I'm not telling you that every single sentence should be short, choppy, and punchy. Obviously, in copy, just like in speech, in talking, you want it to vary, right? And so like just like in the real world, it varies. Sometimes you'll say sometimes you'll one word, sometimes two words, sometimes three words, sometimes a little bit longer, right? But when in doubt, you want short, choppy sentences, when in doubt, I will put a period and I'll start something else, right? I'll start another sentence. And so, right, it again, it gives the copy momentum, as well as it makes it easier to consume. And so short, choppy sentences, when in doubt, right, and you'll see this likely tomorrow, somebody's going to give, somebody's going to bring a line of copy, a paragraph, right? And you're going to see how oftentimes what is three sentences becomes seven sentences for me and words get eliminated, right? Because we want punch to it. We want, we want movement to it, right? And so short, choppy sentences. And then of course, the same thing carrying over, and that is short paragraphs. So what you don't want is you don't want these big chunks of copy, especially, I would say that this applies especially to long form copy, but it also applies to VSLs. One of the things that I tell folks with VSLs just as a side note is you are much better off having less copy per slide. If you're using the typical ugly video sales letter with words, you know, black text on a white background, you're much better off having more slides with less text on each slide than you are having chunks of copy on a single slide. And that's also because even with the VSL, visually, we can create more movement. I'd rather have on a VSL things changing where it's constantly changing. You know, when it comes to the visual medium, you know, you got to like recognize that forget what the the the data shows or the studies show, but the the study show like on most television shows, like the camera angle changes every few seconds, like there's movement every few seconds. It's very rare that something on visual medium stays stagnant, you know, for an extended period of time, even on news programs, there's a reason why they've got a ticker running across the bottom, there's always movement, it's never just this static screen for an extended period of time. And so we're able to we're able to to leverage that movement with a VSL, but we're also able to leverage the feel and vibe of movement and pace with short choppy sentences. You also want short paragraphs because you want it to you want you want you don't want the copy to be intimidating to consume. In other words, you want it easy on the eye. That's why, by the way, breaking up things with subheads, part of that, right, is for the skimmers and scanners, the dual readership path, right, giving you know, giving yourself the ability to pull people back in. But we also use subheads to break up large chunks of copy that look intimidating. And so you want short paragraphs when in doubt, right, like when in doubt, boom, go to go to another paragraph, like, don't worry about is this another topic and should this be another paragraph in itself in itself, right, like, I would try not to go beyond like three lines of of of text, right, break it up, make it easy to consume. Now, all of this really leads to which is already mentioned, but leads to a great piece of advice that I got very early on also from Mark Ford, and that is on the FK score, the Fleischkenkade readability test. So Mark, he told me the story. This was really interesting. So over dinner one night, we were having this dinner, it was Mark, and it was, it was me, and it was Kevin Rogers, and Paris on propolis, and David Deutsch. I can't remember who else was one other person there, I believe. Anyway, we Mark was talking about this is fascinating or should be fascinating to you. Mark was talking about he was two things as it relates to this, and simple words, short choppy sentences and short paragraphs really lead to the FK score. What Mark said was over at the agoracompanies, looking across the board, he looked at the most successful newsletters, the ones that people, the ones that have the highest renewal rate, where at the end of the year, people would renew their subscription. Now, what Mark was referring to were newsletters that were giving financial advice, so investing tips, stock tips, you know, option tips, all that kind of stuff. And he said, what do you think led to the highest renewal rate, right? Now, really, what I would, what I would think, and what I did, what I, what I thought was those that had the best track record in terms of stock tips, the ones that were giving, had the most winners, had the biggest growth in their portfolio, you would think that that's what would lead to the highest renewal rate. That's not what Mark said. What Mark said was that the ones that had the lowest FK score, meaning the ones that were the easiest to read, the easiest to consume, the most enjoyable to consume, had the the ones with the lowest FK score, had the highest renewal rate. Mark also said that as when he, whenever he critiques copy, and when he was copy chiefing, you know, and growing agorah, and, and, and overseeing the copy, anytime a piece of copy, a promotion was sent to him to critique, to edit, to give his feedback on, the very first thing that he did was he ran it through the FK score. And if it was not under eight at the most, if it was not under eight, he would send it back. He would not, he would not review it. He would not look at it. He would not change anything. The very first thing that he required was that the FK score on the copy was below an eight. And there have been, there have been things that Mark has written where he talked about below a seven. And so seven, eight is now what the Fleisch Kinkade readability test is really telling you is how readable is your content, excuse me, is your content, is your copy. And it's basically giving you this grade level. And the reality is, right, is that you want the lowest grade level, you want a low grade level, a grade level of four, a grade level of five. Those are, that is very easy to read copy. Now, most people right away, when they hear about the FK score, they immediately think that means like it, you know, like it is, it's not going to be appropriate for professionals, for doctors, for lawyers, you know, for scientists and whatnot. That's not at all what it means. It's all about the ease of readability, right? And so that's a great point, Lynn, right? That, that, right? Easy reading doesn't mean the underlying ideas aren't, aren't, it doesn't mean that the underlying ideas aren't sophisticated or aren't good. It doesn't mean, it just means that the content, the way that the idea is being articulated is simple and clear. That's the way to look at it. So it doesn't mean that the underlying ideas aren't good. It doesn't mean that the underlying concepts aren't sophisticated. It means that the way that they're being explained is simple and easy enough for that grade level to understand. And that's what you want. So it, like at the end of the day, you know, you could take something simple and make it sound complex through your writing, which is not, which is the opposite of what we want. You could also take something that's complex and sophisticated and present it in a simple way, right? And this is all about words. This is all about the words that you use, the length of the words, how many multi-syllable words are you using, how many adjectives are, you know, are you using, excuse me, adverbs? Are you using L-Y words that end in L-Y? How long are the sentences and how many syllables, how many multi-syllable words are in each sentence? How long, right? How long and, and verbose really is the, is the copy. And so one of the things that I would tell you, this is going to train you to be pithy, to be tight, to be concise, to use simple language, simple words, simple sentence structure by running your copy through the Flage-Concade readability, readability test. I'll tell you that you can use the Hemingway app. I use here, and I'll pop this URL in here, for you this free, this free version. I like this, I'm going to pop this into the chat. This is from Perry Marshall. I love it. You pop your, pop your text in there and see the grade level. And there you go, Scott, man, I got to turn this over to you. I just want to make Scott the presenter at this point. And so, yeah, that's what I use. And really important, like it's one of those things, like I said, one of those things where, you know, like it's got to be simple and easy to understand. And so simple and easy to understand, short, choppy, punchy sentences, read James Altucher, read specifically the essays that he writes. So not the promotional, not the lift note emails, not, you know, not that, not the copy, per se, that, but specifically his essays that are written by James Altucher. You're going to see, like, how simple the, the, the languages, how simple the sentences are, how short they are, in terms of the amount of words in there. You'll see how, how sparse, like, how sparingly he uses commas in the, in there, because more often than not, he's ending it. You'll see how the sentences are like, you know, one subject, one action, you know, result, boom, next one, one subject, one act, result, like very, very simple. And so low FK score. Next, let's go into word choice. So word choice, choosing the right word, the right words to focus on, the words to emphasize, the words to drive your copy might seem insignificant, but, and there are so many quotes like this, but the right word, one word change can change the entire feel of a sentence, right? And one single word change can, can change everything, right? One simple word change can change everything because it only takes one word to, one word to, to change the feel and vibe and the emotion being triggered. One simple word. It takes only one simple word to, to, again, to increase the impact or decrease the impact of something that you are, you are saying. And so don't dismiss this as like, I'm just going to let it, I'm going to let it flow because word choice matters. Now, first and foremost, in terms of word choice, the thing that I want to emphasize for you is this to me is the single most valuable word or words, you know, phrasing, phrasiology that you can use in your copy. And that's you, yours, you are, you will, this ensures that you are focusing on the, on the prospect, right? Like, and so, you know, I, if you look at my copy, and let's do this, I want to show you something here. If you look at my copy, let's do this. Let's do this. Let's do this. Bring this bad boy up. What I want you to see is, I want you to see the, I want you to see the, the percentage of or the ratio of times that I say I and me versus the times that I say you and your. So over here, you can see this may sound crazy. It's working like nothing I've, I've seen before. So there's the first right, I choose any vote from any other, you can borrow it to fill your business with new, new customers, you keep 100% of the money, you can use this with, you can use this, pick any one, borrow it, your business with a surge of fresh new buyers, you can, you can do it for as long as you want to continue to, to drive more and more sales. However, you want, it's the perfect method for you. If you are short on time, don't ever product yet, don't feel or just want to have to do, you, this gets you new customer sales, nothing you've seen before, you can do this no matter what niche you're in, no matter what you sell or you're a new entrepreneur, a grisly veteran, you don't, you don't, you don't, you don't over here. They'll love you for it. You can start seeing, you may want to jump on this now, before your competitors beat you to the punch for your type of, right? My point in sharing that with you is that this whole thing is focused on the prospect, right? And I go back to the, the, the prospect, you, your, you will, you'll see as often as I possibly can. I want to talk about me, my, the least amount that I possibly can, right? That's really what it is that I'm, that I'm looking for. And so, and I'm also, and this is important for you, I am sensitive to sentences where I'm talking about me, or I'm talking about they, like they, the masses out there. And I, I'm sensitive to those. I look to see it, do I have the ability to turn it into a sentence about the prospect? And so, right, like, let me give you, let me show you this. Let's do this. Let's bring this over here. So this was from earlier, right? So, you could say, I'm about to show you, right? Well, that's about me. And that's focused on, right, even though I'm about to show you, right, it's something I am about to do, right? And so, I could certainly take this and say, you're about to see, right? That's about the prospect, right? And so, I, right, I want to focus on the prospect. I want to focus on them. Even things like this is, this is how whatever entrepreneurs can reduce their overhead, right? I could certainly just take this and say, I want to be careful that, right? So I'm talking about entrepreneurs. I'm talking about this nebulous third party out there. I don't want to talk about the third, this third party out there. I want to talk about the prospect. And so, this is simple, right? This is how you can reduce your overhead, right? Understand that this is what the prospect cares about. They don't care about how entrepreneurs can reduce their, why am I talking about this nebulous mass, right? I might be talking to entrepreneurs or to an entrepreneur, right? But what's going to get them excited is not about how entrepreneurs in general, this mass, the they, the people over there, can reduce their overhead, right? This is how they can reduce, this is how you can reduce your overhead, right? And so, I am going to use you, your, you will, you'll see as often as I can. I'm talking about them, their business, their existence, their pain, their outcome, their result. And so, my copy, if you read it, is filled with you, yours, you will, you will, you will, you will, you will. And so, and so, so Guillermo, so yeah, so Guillermo said, when do you replace you and your, when Facebook doesn't like the heavy use of it and will disapprove ads? So, that is a really a completely different, a different topic, right?
