Will get you flagged. And, and so, that's when I'm going to typically third party it, right? So, instead of saying how you can over here, that's when I'm saying this or even better for compliance, I would say, and this is something else that we're going to cover are reducing. This is how entrepreneurs are reducing their overhead, right? And so, that's third partying it and that's not a promise. It's more positioned as a case study of results. But when I bring folks over to our campaign, right, I'm going to get as close to you and yours are in terms of my copy. And so, that is the first, the foundation is you and yours. Next, and this is probably the biggest single most important lesson as it relates to the difference between crappy copy and good copy. And that is this, this next lesson. And that is, hang on one second. There we go. Weak copy is driven by adjectives and adverbs. Good copy is driven by good verbs. And so, what most people, most marketers, most new copywriters think of when they think of good copy, like we talked about yesterday, is not good copy at all. Is hyperbolic exaggerated, overblown, puffed up copy that is loaded with adjectives and adverbs. Adjectives and adverbs don't make for good copy, they make for bloated copy, they make for puffed up copy. What makes for good copy is verbs and good verbs. When your copy is verb driven, when it's action driven, right, like when there is an outcome, when there is a result, when there is movement, good verbs, having verb, having copy that is verb driven, not adjective driven, not adverb driven. Adjectives describe nouns, subjects, adverbs describe verbs. And so, when you see things like the steroid-like explosive, you know, tsunami way for driving leads, that is an example of adjective driven. The steroid-like adjective, powerhouse adjective, amazing adjective, way, right, subject noun to drive, you know, leads. All of that stuff, that powerhouse, tsunami, steroid-like explosive, amazing, all that, that's the puffery part, right? That you're going to understand, that's the puffery part, that is the bloat part. It's the good verbs, it's the drive leads, drive more leads, pump more leads, you know, push more leads into your pipeline, enjoy more sales, see more sales. That's verb driven, right, see more sales, enjoy more sales. If we go back to, I want to show you something here. Let me see this, let's see if I can bring this up. I want to show you something here, see if I can do this quickly. Darn it. Teddy, if you're on and you could message me the link to the SIN offer promo, that would be awesome if you're on and you're listening. So number one, what you're going to see is, and I want you to get this. This is also insanely powerful. This is one of my favorite kind of tactical things. It's covered later on, might as well cover it right now. I call this pluralizing the verb. It's probably, it's probably a better, it's probably an inaccurate way of saying it, but one of the things that you'll notice here is eight things having a send offer does for you in your marketing. Number one, the one thing that I want you to see here, I want you to pay attention to this is, right, that what this is conveying is that the send offer does this for you. This is what I call the power of a passive benefit, meaning this is where you can reap the reward without actually doing the work where the thing does the work for you, right? So this is the difference between, and if I do this to show you, I'm going to show you this. Let me see something here. Where is this right here? So let me, I'm going to tie this together. You're going to see how this ties together. Just bear with me. So there was many moons ago, there, legend has it, that a cassette manufacturer, right? So the old time, like, I don't remember whether it was TDK, that they had an ad, and the headline of the ad said, put music in your life. And then they ran a, another version, and the, the, the, the, when they ran it again, the ad as legend has it, the ad got a 400% boost in response. But there was a, what they claim or the legend claims, there was a typo of one letter, and that one letter, that one typo, as the legend goes, increased response by 400%. And that one typo was the S after put. And so puts music in your life. And so this puts music in your life is, was the, the, the change that produced this 400% increase in response. And the difference between these two is that this one, this is conveying that you do the work, you put magic, or excuse me, music in your life. This is conveying a passive benefit that the tape, the thing puts music in your life. So both get more music in your life. But one is the work is being done by the thing. This is very similar to burn 10 pounds of fat, which is you have to do it, you burn the, the, the 10 pounds of fat versus this thing burns 10 pounds of fat. This burns 10 pounds of fat. You're still experiencing the loss of 10 pounds of fat, but the beauty is that you are the recipient of the benefit, right? So it's passive. Same thing with get more clicks versus it gets you more clicks. So this is all about this idea of, right, get more clicks, right? Like, I'll show you how to use this to get more clicks. I'll show you how to use this to get more clicks. That is still a benefit, right? But it's the prospect doing the work of getting more clicks versus I'll show you how to use this with, which gets you more clicks is the thing this is doing the, the work for you. And so this is incredibly powerful when you have a thing that, right, you can position the thing as doing the work, the thing is doing the heavy lifting and the prospect as the passive recipient of the work that the thing is doing, the passive recipient of the benefit, that's insanely powerful because it makes me feel like I'm just going to sit back and I'm going to reap all of these, all of these benefits. And now if you look at this right here, you can see number one, you'll see the s, the s, the s, the s, the s, the s, the s, the s, right here, the s, right here, right? All right. So this is the eight things having a send offer does for you. So this is right explicitly saying it does this for you. Now look at how this is all driven. It proves what is it? It's a verb proves demonstrates makes generates brings decreases makes increases, right? And so it proves why prospects spend proves why prospects should spend more money with you demonstrates why you're the superior choice in your market makes it a no brainer for prospects to say yes, you generate more revenue for you from new sales brings you more new clients, buyers decreases your right, it's action. It does this, it does this, it does this, it does this, it does this, it does this. And so what we are talking about here is we are talking about verb driven, verb driven copy, verb driven action driven, not loaded with it doesn't right not not loaded with adverbs and adjectives. You'll notice that there was no descriptor before decreases drives makes increases, right? It was the verb I let the verb do the work, right? Because verb gives movement, it gives action. And so we want verb driven copy. Now to add to that, right to add to that, I want you to understand that I want you to understand that what we're looking for good verbs, not all verbs are good, not all verbs convey movement. And so there are some like, and you'll understand what I mean by good verbs in just a second, but weak verbs are vague, they're fuzzy, they're static, right? And again, there's that word vague, meaning weak verbs are verbs that you can't conjure in your mind, they don't appeal to one or more of the senses, you can't see an action, nothing is, nothing is occurring, they're, they're static, they're kind of being verbs are what they're sometimes referred to or sometimes they're referred to as dead verbs because they're not alive, there's no movement happening. So a verb like have do get show, show, show, oops. No idea why there's another thing that I'm supposed to have in there, but use, right? Like, I, I, you know, I use the hammer. What does that mean? What does that look like? Or I have a hammer, right? What does that look like? Is it right? I held the hammer. Is it I grip the hammer? Is it I squeeze the hammer? Is it I slam the hammer? It right? Like, you know, what, what is the action going on? Right? And so when you say, right, like, I have, you know, I have XYZ, there's no action, there's no movement, it's vague, it's fuzzy, I can't picture anything in, in my mind's eye, right? And so remember, we are creating mental pictures, we're conjuring images, that's where always emotion flows from mental images. And so what we're looking for is we're looking for good verbs, we're looking for verbs that demonstrate movement, that demonstrate action. And so good verbs, let me see here, let me scroll this down, move this down. So good verbs, like I said, appeal to one or more of the sentences. It's said, good verbs invite us to see, hear, touch, taste, smell, objects and ideas, right? And so good verbs convey visual imagery and action. Good verbs demonstrate motion and activity. And so, you know, it's important to understand that what you're, you're looking to, right, like, when you say, you know, if you use the word like, get more traffic to your website, right? Well, is get more traffic, is get a visual word, right? Like get more traffic into your, into your marketing. Is that a good word or is pump more, drive more, attract more? Because even a word like attract more, right? Attract the message that it sends, even the visual idea of luring them in, lore is even, right, is a verb, is, you know, has more imagery than just get, right? Get is like just a flat, it's like, and so, and so we're looking for, like, we're looking for, for verbs that convey movement that appeal to one or more of the sentence to the senses. And you've got to recognize that different words have a different impact, right? Like to even say, and I share this, and we're going to, I'm going to, I'm going to get to this in just a second. But like, if there was any place where you want to use a thesaurus, it is going back and looking for better verbs, verbs that paint more of a picture, verbs that have more power, have more oomph, add more feel to it, right? So you could say, you know, Bob, Bob hit Tom, right? Bob hit Tom. But there's a lot of, you know, Bob hit Tom. That doesn't tell us, did he hit him hard? Did he hit him, you know, did he hit him softly, right? Did he hit him with anger? Did he hit him with rage? Like to say, you know, you know, whatever, Bob hit Tom, you know, Bob slug Tom, you know, Bob, you know, Bob crack Tom in the face, you know, Bob hammered Tom, right? Like, you know, Bob kick Tom. You know, there are so many different ways to add more, right? To say that Bob hit Tom. Yes, that's action. Yes, that's a verb. Yes, there's movement there. But there is right away to add more flair to it with the right verb or better verbs. Also, when you, when you find yourself using adverbs, adverbs are the L Y like words, right? Like to describe adverbs describe a verb. So adverb like, like, for example, slowly walk, right? Slowly walk is the use of a is a verb walk, and then a descriptor for the verb for walk slowly. So when you find that you are using verbs and you're incorporating these L Y words in there to describe to better describe the verb, the action, that it should be a signal for you to look for a better singular verb. This is how you'll have tighter copy. You'll have punchier copy, and you'll have better copy. So rather than say slowly walk, right? It's strut rather than say gently touch, right? Touch being the verb, gently being the adverb, the descriptor for it, caress, loudly yell, scream, vigorously push, shove, right? I know that this might seem, you know, like, this is one of those things where most marketers don't like to hear this, don't, you know, they're bored by this. This is like, that, you know, like, I'm not interested in this, this is too tedious. But this is the difference between, right, like, good copy or good enough copy and great copy. This is the difference between like conversions and great conversions. This is the difference between this is a profitable campaign, and this is a grand slam home run. It's all of the little things that add up because this gives the copy feel, it gives it momentum, it gives it movement. And there's an experience that prospects have when consuming this kind of copy. That's just different. It's more enjoyable than is the the run of the mill stuff that people are putting out there. The beauty is that in some cases, just this type of change, just this focus on verbs and tightening up the copy short, short simple words, short choppy sentences, shorter paragraphs that are driven by verbs that aren't filled with adjectives and adverbs. The overall feel and experience itself conveys differentiation. There's something different about the way that this person is communicating, the way that they sound, the way that they feel, the way that they're articulating and communicating their message that just feels better than and more exciting than the other copy, right? And it was, you know, Chris Cole on our, on my team who is, I believe, still on here, he asked me also, you know, he asked me that question that I mentioned earlier about, you know, he said there's this excitement in, you know, in copy. You know, there's this excitement in the copy. And where does that come from? And I said, first and foremost, the foundation of it comes from starting with an excitement about the idea, right? But it also comes from not using dead verbs, not right? So there's the copy almost feels alive. It feels more exciting because it feels like there's movement, there's things happening, things are, are, there's action, right? There's action occurring because of the word choice, right? So rather than, you know, get, it's grab, it sees, right? You know, and so right, get more traffic, get more leads, get more sales. That's such a flat way of saying it, get more sales, right? Pump more sales. Like there's just so many different ways to make that more action oriented rather than those, those, you know, those, those flat words. And even verbs like is and was, those are flat dead verbs, right? Bob was behind the counter. Right? Bob was behind the counter. What does that mean? What was he doing? Was he just standing there? If Bob was behind the counter? Bob work behind the counter, right? Bob hid behind the counter, you know, you know, whatever of Bob, Pace, behind the counter, there's so many different ways to rather than bob was behind the counter. That is, right? Was he just standing behind a counter? Was he lurking behind the counter? Right? Was he was he pacing behind the counter? Was he, you know, hiding behind the counter? There are so many different ways to add movement. And that movement adds a vibe, adds momentum to the copy. Next is what I want you to understand. And this is these are all little finer points to just be aware of. This is going to be a lot. This is why I said you're going to want to go back to the recordings. And this is going to be why you want these PDFs to go and review this. When I send this to you tomorrow, but I want you to understand the difference between past and present tense verbs and why why this matters. So let's say you used the word. Right. How we grew. How we grew a how we grew our business, let's just say, how we grew our business from zero, you know, to a hundred thousand dollars or how we built a list of, you know, how we built a list of a million people. Right. We understand that when you talk about benefits and I know that like, like, you know, I want you to get the gist of this, that I want to talk about things in the present. I don't want to talk about things in the past because it gives prospects the ability to think, oh, that's old. That happened already. Maybe it's no longer effective. Maybe it no longer works. Right. So how you grew your business from zero to a million people or how you grew your list from zero to a million people using Facebook. Well, maybe it's no longer effective. And maybe that's why you're telling me or maybe things have changed so radically since what it is that you did, right? That that that like it's not going to be valuable anymore. And so it's not grew. It's not how entrepreneurs, how these entrepreneurs grew their business from zero to. It's how they're growing. It's how they're driving. It's how they're getting its present tense. It's not how they scaled. It's how they're scaling. It's now one implies that it was done like that it's old. One implies that it's working now, that it's effective now, that this is happening now. And this little change in intense matters because people are are scared off by old ideas, even when those old ideas were effective. Right. What people want to know is now what's working now, what's effective now. And so we're very sensitive to it's not, you know, how we scaled our business. It's how we're scaling our business. It's not how we're it's not how we produce 10 percent, 10 percent, a consistent 10 percent sales conversion rate. It's how we're producing a consistent 10 percent sales conversion rate. It's not how we drove. You know, we drove thousands of leads. It's how we're driving thousands of leads. Seems like an insignificant difference. It's not at all. It's not how we saved on our, you know, we save 50 percent on our on our our CPA. It's how we're saving or how we save, you know, 50 percent on our CPA. It's not how we got, right? Like, you know, crazy profits. It's how we're getting crazy profits. That difference in tense matters. It matters because it conveys that this is happening now. And that's what your prospects want. We already talked about pluralizing the the verb. Next, let's talk about adjectives. So I said good copy is driven by verbs. It's not driven by by adjectives. Does that mean that all adjectives are bad? No, it does not mean that all adjectives are bad. When we use adjectives, what is important is that you recognize that only use adjectives when they add more information, right? So when you add a descriptor to, you know, when you when you describe. So remember when we said, you know, the amazing, incredible steroid like, you know, muscle building machine, blah, blah, blah. All of those are are are adjectives, right? Only use adjectives when it adds more information. Make sure that you're adding more information to the to the to the noun, to the to the subject, like, right? You want to use an adjective when you want to give more information, when you want to give more detail. Why would you want to give more information? Why would you want to give more detail? A clear, crisper mental image when it when you want them to conjure an image, right? And we need to give more detail. Give it, but don't add an adjective just as a filler word or as puffery. In other words, avoid those fluff adjectives, those really meaningless adjectives, right? Like the amazing, incredible, the, the, the superlative, the amazing, incredible, fantastic, superb, outstanding, great, excellent, extraordinary, awesome, top shelf, supreme, striking. Those are all examples. And there's many, many more of meaningless adjectives, right? The amazing system. What additional information do we give the prospect when we say the amazing system? What, what additional information do they know that they didn't know before? Right? When we, the, the, the, the, the awesome system, the awesome way to blah, blah, blah. What additional information have we given them? All we've done is we've bloated the copy. All we've done is added in information, right? Like more, excuse me, more words that don't give any more information. And so when in doubt, right? When in doubt, take out the adjective unless you understand the, the, the, the, what the additional information that it is adding. And so let me see here. And so another thing is, right? Sometimes like you want to make sure that you are choosing the, the most like, like, look, again, the L Y, right? Like instead of really giving, he's generous, right? And so my point is tight copy, tight copy. Next to continue. Yeah, when in doubt, take it out, man. Like the reality is this, it's so funny how most people, when they think of editing, they're copy, they think of going back in and plugging in the stuff that's missing. When the reality is, is the way that I think about it is trimming the stuff that's unnecessary. Right? It's trimming the stuff that's unnecessary. And so I'm getting rid of as much as I can. And I'm going to show you how to do that with certain words even that are unnecessary. Next is you want evocative language, not emotive language. The difference between these two, in other words, what you want to do, you, you don't want to emote, you want to evoke. You don't want to share your emotion or display your emotion. You want to spur their emotion. It's about stimulating your prospects emotions, not sharing yours. Right? It's not about you being excited or you demonstrate. You're not, you're not, you're not showing how excited you are. You're not showing how, how pumped you are. You're not showing how, right? How like you're getting them excited. Our job is to, is to evoke, not emote. It's not about our emotion. It's about the prospects emotion. It's about getting them emotional, not us demonstrating how emotional we are. So, right? Even things like I'm so excited to tell you versus you'll be excited when you hear, right? Not only is one about me, right? I am excited versus you will, but one is I'm so excited. Well, I'm the, I'm the prospect, right? I'm the prospect that I'm reading about this, this person telling me they're so excited. Why do I care that you're excited? I don't really care that they're excited. Tell me about me. Talk about me. Right? Like, um, it's the, it's the, the classic, right? Uh, enough about me. Tell me about you. How do you feel about me? Right? Like that. Like it at the end of the day, you want to evoke emotion from them, not emote your own emotion. Right? Not emote your own emotion. And so, um, and when, um, when possible, this is right? A little, you want to pair evocative, uh, um, uh, sensory language when talking about their pain. Right? Like, so when we, when we say, um, you know, the, when we're talking about their, um, like pain, when we're talking, when we're, when we're generating contrast, right? You can take that, um, evocative language and you compare it with, um, an experiential, uh, experience, like no more sitting in silence. So no more feeling anxious on calls. Right? I don't remember where this came from, where I saw this, right? But this is, that's an example of, right? You're, you're talking about negative. It's, you know, contrast providing, you know, providing, you know, talking about the negative side of things and it's sensory, no more sitting in silence, no more sitting in silence, no more feeling anxious. And so, um, and so, um, yeah, so evocative, not, uh, you want to evoke, not emote. And Scott, if you have anything to add, if this is a favorite subject of yours, please fill in any gaps for, um, for our peeps. If you feel that there's anything that, um, that I trimmed from this, um, you know, added into the, um, added into the chat, I'd love it. Next, right? We're, we're on word choice. So we're talking about choose when the words that you choose should evoke emotion, not emote, um, emotion, right? And so evoke, not emote. Next is, this is like, it might, again, maybe might seem like nuance, right? But it's not nuance. And it is the use of the word and the word, but, and so let me explain what I mean here in this context, because sometimes what I see is I see copy that says something like builds up this excitement or builds up this tension or builds up this payoff and then follows it up that negates what it is that they just said. And so you've got to be careful because the use of the word, but which sometimes is natural in our, in our speech, sometimes the use of the word, but really more often than not, the use of the word, but negates what was just said prior. So if I said, this is super simple to use, but it's extremely powerful, right? It rather than say, but this is where I'm going to say, this is super simple to use. And it's also extremely powerful. You have to be sensitive to, if I add in the word, but right, am I negating what it is that I just said? Am I putting these things in, in contrast to each other? See, in, in this sentence, what I'm really trying to say here, right? What I'm really trying to say here is that this thing is super simple and it is also though, right? Extremely powerful. I'm not trying to negate. Like I'm not trying to add, right? I'm not trying to get, I'm trying to pile on. This is addition, not subtraction. And so, right? It's this and it's this. And so be careful and be sensitive to, to, to when you use, to when you use each next. See here. So real quick, I'm going to take a question before I go on to this next little chunk. So Shane said the question is from when I talk about short punchy senses and maybe it's trivial, trivial, but I see the dot, dot, dot to lead to the next sentence. Is there a time and a place or should I not worry much about it? Novice here. Michael said you may cover this shortly so I can wait. No, I wouldn't really get so, Shane, it's a great question. I love the question. All questions, everybody starts off as a, you know, in the same spot as a novice, right? At one point, we all knew nothing about copy. And so we all start in the same place. And so deep admiration, man, for you, we're at right now. And so, no, I wouldn't get too, too caught up in it. Sometimes what I like, I will, I typically use the ellipsis where more often than not, where there's more, there's more to come. Like I'm continuing this thought. But if I want to continue the thought on the next line, like I might say this is, this is extremely powerful. And maybe on the next line, I want to explain why. And I broke it up because the, the, the explanation is a little bit longer. And I didn't want it all on, on one line. So to me, this is extremely powerful. I don't want to put a period there because I don't want them, I don't want them to stop yet without getting the proof, without getting the support, because I don't want them to stop. Right. They'll, they'll see a period. They'll stop and then they'll process and be like, that's a claim. That's a little bogus. So I don't want them to prematurely stop. I don't, I don't want them to prematurely look at that as a complete thought. Because for me, it's not. So I might say this is extremely powerful dot, dot, dot because of the way that it works to do. So I'm using the ellipsis just as a way of demonstrating a continue in thought that this isn't the end of the thought. And so, right, sometimes I'll do that on a, on a, on a line, like without, without creating a new paragraph. Sometimes I'll create a new paragraph just because of the format, just because of the bulk of the copy, just because I don't want that much copy in one, in one sentence. And so, but it, it demonstrates a, a continuance in thought for me so that I, I prevent the prospect from prematurely trying to process the information without all of the information. Right. And so hopefully that, that makes sense. And so, but I wouldn't get too, too caught up in that. Talk a little bit, to talk a little bit about if and when. This is just a slight tweak that you can make that just adds a little bit of a little bit of, of a different, a difference in impact. And that is this, and not all the time, right? But you can replace if statements with when statements. So oftentimes what I see is, is, you know, you'll, you will see things like, if you struggle, if you struggle to burn fat from your waist, or if you struggle to turn website traffic into sales. If you, you know, if you struggle to, to element OP, right, you can very simply, right, turn it from if your struggling to get new buyers, right. It, right, to when you're struggling to get new buyers, when you're struggling to get new buyers, right, your whole business is at risk versus saying if you're struggling to get new buyers, your whole business is at risk. It's a slight shift. Right. But it is a valuable shift when it, when it is appropriate, right? So rather than saying if you're struggling to, if you're struggling to get new leads into your campaigns, right, you need to hear this when you're struggling to get new new leads into your campaigns, nothing is more important than this. And so if verse when, this is another biggie that I've become really sensitive to over the, I would say over the last handful of years in just in my effort to, in my effort to really become, you know, pithy tight. And that is the word that the word that so in many, many cases, especially in the, the written word. So that's one thing, JMC, we're gonna, we're gonna talk about the word that becoming the word which but more often than not, you could trim unnecessary, the use of the word that so many times the word that is just unnecessary. So here's a great example. And now there's nothing wrong grammatically with this sentence, right? Like this is a sentence that I would have written, right? Like he said that he was going to the store so that he could get some food, right? He said that he was going to the store so he could get some food. There's nothing wrong with that sentence, right? But you don't need that those two instances of the word that, right? And so you could take it out. Now look, let me like, look, is this make or break stuff? Like, is this like, you know, is this make or break stuff? It's not make or break stuff, but you do this repeatedly over and over and over in your copy. And it's going to feel different, right? You have excessive words in your copy over and over and over and over. And, and it's going to have a different feel than tight, punchy, short, consumable copy that's easy to get through. And so you, there's no, right? He said that he was going to the store so that he could get some food. He said he was going to the store so he could get some food. You don't need the word that in there. And I'm very, very sensitive to this, even in my own, in my own writing constantly, right? Like eliminating the word, the word that when it's unnecessary, when I don't need it. And so one becomes, right? He said that he was going to the store so that he could get some food versus he said he was going to the store so he could get some food. Like you see how like it's tighter, it's tighter. You can also, when you do need to use the word that, when you do need to word, to use the word that you can replace it with the word which you can, you can do more. And that means that you get faster results. You can do more, which means you get faster results. Now what I want you to see here is this, right? You can do more. And that means that you get faster results. This was an unnecessary that I didn't need this in here, right? And I could replace that with which. So, and I actually replaced two words with one word and got rid of this. So you can do more and that means that you get faster results. You can do more, which means you get faster results. See short, punchy, chop, boom, boom, boom. And so when in doubt, take out the word that, right? Don't, you know, take it out. When you do need it, see if you can use the word which in there. And so next is emotionally charged words. This is something that you're going to have to spend time and get better at. This is going to require effort on your part initially, right? To become more sensitive to this, but different words carry a different emotional charge, meaning different words, some words are more emotionally charged, elicit a more visceral response than other words, right? And I'm not talking about, you know, I'm talking about words that are conveying the same core underlying idea. Different words can convey a different level of emotion. So one of the best examples was, I believe this comes from John Carlton, the difference between the word embarrassed and the word humiliate, right? Like there's a different emotion, there's a different visceral response to the word humiliate compared to the word embarrassed. And so like if I was, was, was writing a piece of copy and I was writing a sentence in there where I wanted to trigger this, like this, you know, this, this idea of no longer, you know, no longer be embarrassed, no longer feel self conscious. Like I wanted to create this extreme contrast between, right, that bad feeling, that negative feeling and the really good feeling of confidence and, and what not to be in shape, right? Well, I could say no longer feel embarrassed taking your shirt off, right? Versus blah, blah, blah, feel confident. Or I could also to really ramp up or amp up that emotional response, no longer feel humiliated when you take your shirt off. And so, right, that's the example of changing the word to drive up the emotional, emotional response. And so you've got to recognize you've got to start to look at different words and which one comes with more power. You've got to recognize that there's power in your words. And right, like what you say, right, you might say one word and it might trigger somebody like to see it, like even, even different words, as we're going to talk about create a different frame for people. And so take, they, you know, they, they, they, they, you know, they take money from the cash register versus they swipe money from the cash register, right? Like you see the two, two words, they take money from the cash register, they swipe money from the register. One, right, brings much more of a visceral response. They swipe money like those mother, you know, like they're getting away with it, right? Like verb, but a different verb has a different vibe, right? Beat or pummeled, right? Like they're conveying some, a same, really the same core underlying idea, but one is pummeled is more severe than beat. One, or they dominated. They won the game. They dominated the game, right? One, certainly you, right? One, the dominated is conveying this like it was a breeze, it was easy, right? And my point is that you've got to understand like there's a different emotional impact. And so I want the peak emotional impact that I can, I can convey tired or exhausted, made fun of or mocked, right? You think about this, right? Like somebody said, right, like I was made fun of in school or I was mocked in school, right? Like mocked just feels like it stimulates that like, right? Like, you know, and so if we were talking about, you don't have to, right, deal with being made fun of, you don't have to deal with being mocked anymore, right? Like different emotional response. And so what you're looking for, again, not to be a, you know, not, not to be a broken record here, but you're looking for the word that conveys the most amount of emotional charge, right? And so you want to make sure of course, of course that you're using words that, that elicit the emotions that you want, but then you want to choose the most emotionally charged word that you can, broke, crushed. He told, you know, he told on him, he snitched on him, right? Like, I love the difference between this. And Mike said, you know, and Mike is absolutely right. Read more fiction. You know, that's why I like, right, like I read fiction to really, for the writing to see the way they describe things, to see the way they paint and convey movement, right? It's a lot less about like, it's a lot less about enjoyment really honestly, than it is for me, than it is paying attention to how good writers, right, paint pictures. And the beautiful thing is sometimes I read fiction and I'm like, this isn't really good, right? And then I ask myself, well, why isn't this good? And it's not good because like, like it's, it's average every day descriptions, right? Like there's a lot of different and I look at that and I'm like, you know what, that, like, I got to learn from that. I don't learn from that. And so understand that there is a difference in emotional charge in words. And, and when you are, you're looking for the high, the most amount of emotional charge. And so not every word is the, is the same. This is really somewhat tied into the idea of loaded language. And loaded language, the kind of the official definition of this loaded language is a phrase, a phrase or word is loaded, when it has a secondary evaluative meaning, meaning, in addition to its primary core, you know, meaning into, right? When there is a, right? So loaded language, like loaded language, like, you know, and this is somewhat of a rhetorical kind of device. You see this often. And this is something for you to just be aware of, right? Who is great at this? Who is great to a fault, great in a negative way at this are politicians, right? Politicians are great at this, because what they can do is they can take anything, right? And they can use loaded language to change the entire frame, to change the way we look at things, right? So you've got, think of the difference between, you know, between these two, right here, right? And forget, put aside your personal feelings, all that, like, we're not, we're talking about copy right now. And so nobody come down on me for any of this. Right? So pro abortion versus pro choice, you're still talking about the same individuals, right? But no one describes it as pro abortion, right? And there's, right? No one describes it as pro abortion, right? They describe it as pro choice, right? They're changing the frame, right? They change the entire frame of that. It's really unbelievable from a persuasion and a marketing perspective. And I know I believe there is, I believe Scott is probably a, your Scott is probably a pro at this, if I'm not mistaken, if I recall correctly. Yeah, when it certainly did. And so be it. So be it. But you know, it's amazing how the, yeah, that's, I mean, a great way to, like a great way to put it, like, it's, right, let's just, let's, let's change the, let's change the frame with how we, we, we describe it, right? You could say, right? Like weed versus plant, you know, how you describe the word, right? Like, you know, like comes with a, a pre-established connotation. You could refer to something as an insect or you could also refer to something as a pest. Now you'll notice that, right? We could be talking about the same thing, but the word pest, right? The word pest has a secondary connotation in there, right? And so that's a negative connotation. If I wanted to stir up a negative emotion when talking about insects, well, I'm going to call them pests rather than insects, because it elicits that a deeper, a negative emotional response because of the secondary evaluative meaning that comes with it. Just like, right? We might be talking about weeds, but weeds are plants, right? But weeds have a negative connotation. Weeds are unwanted. A plant could be a positive thing. A weed is never a positive thing. And so, right? I'm not talking about weed, that kind of weed here, right? I'm talking about weeds in your backyard or whatever, on your patio or whatever, right? And so same thing, how about this? I love this. This is where I've got to keep myself from getting political with this. But I love the people that, right? And I don't care what you do. I have friends that are pro-vaccine. I have friends that are anti-vaccine. I don't really care. I love them all just the same. I might not agree with their stance, their perspective, but God bless you, do you, whatever you want to do. And I'm cool with it, right? But I love the people that, when they talk about, you know, what do they do? They use loaded language intentionally to talk about the jab, the vaccine, and they call it a medical procedure, right? And what I really want to say is get the freak out of here with that, man. Like go away, right? Because that's loaded language, right? It's choosing a certain frame for it, right? Like that becomes a medical procedure. They'll use medical procedure to talk about that and not, right? And I'm not right wrong or indifferent, right? It's loaded language and you just have to have to recognize that. And there's, right? The thing to recognize is that loaded, you know, loaded language can even come in the form of a single word, right? A single word can change the impact, the frame, the meaning of what you're trying to bring about because certain words bring with them, right? Bring with them a secondary evaluative meaning. That secondary meaning could be positive more often than not it's negative, right? But it brings like this, her benefits were slashed versus her benefits were reduced, right? So frame control, framing, like this fancy idea of, oh, I love presuppositions. We're going to talk about that. Frame control and presuppositions, they're all over there, because presuppo... because it works, right? Because it works. And so, but frame control is, right? It is all about like, it's all about impacting, changing, altering your prospect's perception of the facts without ever changing the facts, right? And so the beautiful thing is that frame control is, you could take the same set of facts and you can shine a light on an area of it to make it look one way or feel one way, or you could shine another light on it to make it feel or look another way, right? Another way. That's why. It's a side note. Can I get a little personal for one second? Is it cool if I step out, even though we have 874 slides to go still? And okay. So let me say this, right? Let me do this for one second. Step out of this. Probably should... Nah, I don't need to stop the recording for this because it's not that bad. But let me say this. And so, right? You know what? The reason why you will... the reason why there are these polar opposites in the world, whether it's political, whether it's on, you know, I don't care what the stance is, right? And everybody is trying to compete and argue on facts, right? Here is... here, here is a... let me present you with a fact here. And then one... the person over here, this is like, how do you not see that? And the person over there says, how do you not see this? Like, they're looking at the same exact fact. And that's because everybody processes information, facts included, through a worldview. And that worldview will color how they process and see those facts. And unless the worldview changes, they will never see those facts the same way. And so, every fact can be processed through or is processed through the worldview. And when you have two opposing worldviews, the same exact fact, the same exact fact, could drive two completely different conclusions. So, all this time of... let me... here, here's the fact. The fact is it. The fact is it. The study said it. What you're getting is, you're trying to argue over facts, but yet the worldview will not allow the processing of that. Like, you're going to see it the way you're going to see it. This is why, let's say you take a Christian and you take an atheist. And you'll show them the fossils or whatnot. And you'll say, you see how this is... look at gorillas, look at these systems. Every system in the animal kingdom works the same way, with the stomach, with the digestive tract, with a heart, with a brain, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And the atheist will say, you see, that's a system that we all... you know, that's an example or a sign that we all originated from the same source. Right? And so, see? Evidence. How do you not get that? And yet the Christian will see that and will say, that's because it was all created by the same creator. That's evidence that how do you not see that there is a system, right, that is used there? And so, for each of those two parties, they're looking at the same exact piece of information, but they're processing it through a different worldview. And you will never, ever win when you are trying to process on facts alone, because you have to understand there is a worldview that it is being seen through and processed through. And so, right? That's when I just shut off. And so, right? So, you got to understand frame control, right? Like that, you have the ability to shine a light on what you want the prospect to feel. In other words, we're going to present the information. We're going to talk about things in our copy in a way that elicits the emotion that we want. So, frame control, framing, frame control is all about, right, is all about altering the prospects perception of the information, even though we're not changing the information. So, to me, the best example of this, right, is that, you know, the little tale, the little story of the guy who is about to undergo surgery and who asks the surgeon who says, right, like, how successful is the surgery? And the surgeon says, oh, man, 90% of the people who undergo this surgery are fine, right? That's one frame. That's a positive frame. 90% of the people that undergo the surgery, man, they get off the table, they're good to go. That's one frame if you want to create a positive frame, right? But then you take the other side of the fence and you say, well, how successful is the surgery? Well, 10% of the people who undergo the surgery die on the table, right? One is a positive frame, one is a negative frame. We're dealing with the same underlying information, the same set of facts, but we're shining a light on a different component to change the frame, right? And so, let me show you a simple way that just one way, and this is frame control, framing is involved in everything that we do because we're shining a light on what we want the prospect to see. Even, right, like we're talking about things that we want the prospect to understand so they feel a certain way and we're leaving out the things that we don't and we're presenting it in the angle that we want to present to impact or alter their perception of the fact, right? We're not changing the facts. We're not lying about the facts, but we're shining a light on an aspect. Here is a simple example of frame control and the impact on price presentation. And this is money that you should write down, right? And then I'm going to, if there are any questions, I'll take a, after I do frame control, I'll take a question or questions. And so, this is a great example, right? So, let's say you offer a paid in full and you offer a payment plan. Well, there are two different ways that you can present this. So, the most common way to present this, the way that I used to present, I used to present it all the time, was like this. Let's say you today, right? So, the product, right, you get this for one payment of a thousand bucks. Or if you want, you could jump on a payment plan of just four payments of 300. So, what you'll notice right off the bat is you'll notice that the, in this instance, the payment plan is a totals up to 1200, right? So, you can get the product for a thousand bucks. Or if you want a payment plan, you know on the payment plan, it's just four payments of 300 bucks, which brings it to 1200. So, there's an additional $200 charge. There's an additional $200 charge if you take the payment plan. But exactly like Brian said, you could also do it this way. How much is the product? It's four payments of, you know, it's, you could say it's $1200, right? Split over four payments. $1200, four payments of $300. Or you can save 200 bucks today with only one payment of a thousand bucks. This is huge, right? This is huge because the difference here is this, right? So, the difference here is this, that in the first one, this, right, in the first one, the product is a thousand bucks. And, but if you want, you could get a payment plan, right? You get a payment plan, but there's a penalty of $200, right? And so, you're going to really, in essence, pay $1200 for a thousand dollar product. So, right, you're going to pay $1200 for a thousand dollar product. You're being penalized $200 to go on the payment plan. But in the second example, right, you're really getting a $1200 product and you're getting a savings, a discount today of $200 when you pay in full. And so, one is a reward. One is a penalty, right? And so, this is why you look at when I do a paid in full and a payment plan at the same time, right? There is always a discrepancy in price. If we're going to have to manage a payment plan and follow up with people if their card expires or whatnot, there's always going to be a discrepancy in price between a payment plan and paid in full. But I'm going to present the paid in full as the, as a bonus, as a reward. Like, if you choose to, you could save an additional XYZ today as a reward for making a single payment of just a thousand bucks. So, that's you getting a thousand dollar excuse me, a $1200 product for a thousand bucks. You saving $200 on the price. That is a simple shift in frame. That's all it is in how it's being presented. One penalty, one reward, one a thousand dollar product that you can get for $1200 or one a $1200 product that you can get for a thousand. Simple shift. That's like a simple example of, you know, like, you know, and you see, read my copy. Read my copy and you'll see me say things like, you already know that you need traffic to, right? And so we don't need to spend a whole lot of time here on this, but metaphor metaphor is a rhetorical device. Metaphor was what when I when I had that conversation, that phone call with with Robert Chaldini metaphor was the trigger the the persuasive trigger that he said was the most powerful. And the reason why metaphor is or can be extremely powerful. And there's a lot of different ways to use metaphor. But metaphor is most appropriate when you're talking about a complicated subject. And to simplify it, you use a metaphor to link it to to a known to link it to something that the prospect already understands that the prospect already sees that the prospect already gets that the prospect already has, you know, an established connotation with. So we're taking the complex and we are we're simplifying it by attaching it through metaphor to an already an already known. And so right what you're what you're able to do, right, like is you you're able to take potentially a, you know, like a complex, complex set of information, complex information, and you're able to boil it down in one quick image that makes the person say, you know, like, I get it. The difference between this and again, this is also something that you can look up on on Google. But this is really a matter of the difference between active and passive voice. Let me show you a passive voice sentence. The passengers were asked to return to their seats. So what you can see here is that if you looked at the, you know, were asked, were asked, right, is the, they were asked. This is kind of the action that was taken. And it was taken on the passengers, right? But what this doesn't answer is who was taking the action. So what we're looking at here is we're looking at the impact of an action. In other words, an action was taken by somebody or something on the passengers. The passengers were asked to return to their seats. That is passive voice. It's passive because in this context, and I don't want you to get into the, you don't need to get into the, to the, to the nitty gritty details, right? It's passive because the passengers were the recipients of the action. They were asked to return to their, to their, to their seats. Active is when it is clear, right? Who was taking the action? So we're going to get to that in just a second. Let me show you another passive voice sentence. The ball was kicked into the goal, right? And so passive because the ball received the action, right? But who kicked the ball? Who's doing the action? We don't actually see the action. We're seeing who's performing the action. We just see the passive recipient of the action. Next clicks were generated like crazy, right? Clicks were generated like crazy. That is again, right? There is, there is no, you don't see the, the, the active driver of the action. So in this context, we know that John was the passive recipient of the action, right? Over here and the action taker was Tom, but you don't, there is no, this is not, this is not active voice, active voice, right? Like, like, so passive voice is stagnant. Active voice is movement, right? Is movement. And so let me show you how to, how to fix this. So the passengers were asked to return to their seats. So what we always want to show is, we want to show, remember I said earlier that Brilliant's breakthrough by Eugene Schwartz, great book to get. It talks about subject action, right? Boom. Like subject action outcome, subject action outcome. So right, the cat jumped, you know, Bob spit, you know, John sat, you know, Bill waved, you know, Bill waved goodbye, Bill waved to John or whatnot. There's, there is somebody that is taking an action. There's an action that occurring and there is an outcome. That is the simplest form of sentences. That's the type of short, punchy copy that you're looking for. And so what we're looking for is who is the active participant in the action. And ultimately we want to put the, the active participant of the action before the verb. So who is the active participant? What is the action that they took on who or to who and the result, right? And so take the passengers were asked to return to their seats. The passengers didn't take the action. They were the recipient of the action. We're going to go through, I'm going to go through some storytelling, I'm going to go through some tactics, and then we're going to go through language patterns, which is a deep, awesome, cool, kind of topic that you can use, like topic of stuff that you can just use, swipe, like copy that you can take models of copy that you can take, and just plug and play for different impacts. And so, I want to talk a little bit about stories, because I know that this is something that, that lots of folks are interested in, and excuse me. And so, but I only want to, I'm going to cover this at a very high level. So we're not going to get into the nitty-gritty details of, of storytelling. I've already talked about that stories can be one of the most effective vehicles for marketing, but it's got to be done correctly. And it's a skill that can be learned, but it needs to be learned. And so I just want to cover a couple of tips related to stories, the couple of the big most important tips that, that have been most helpful for me. And so number one is this. And that is start in the middle of the action, right? And so, you know, when it comes to stories for marketing, especially, you see this, first of all, in a lot of action films, you also see this in a lot of really good fiction Lee Child, who I mentioned earlier, who's great person, great author to read for the sentence structure, sentence length, staccato pace, all that, but also really good because he always opens up, yeah, Quentin Tarantino as well, all they always open up in the middle of the action. In other words, you've got to recognize that, you know, it's kind of like, look, you know, you don't want, you know, the, the, the, I remember opening up a Lee Child novel and looking at the first sentence and like the first sentence was something along the lines of, you know, Bob never thought that he would, Bob never thought that he would get shot on the middle of a Tuesday morning. Like that was the very first sentence in the, in the, in the book, right? Like bringing you into the, you know, into the heart of the, into the middle of the action, into the middle of the drama. And so what he, they didn't, he didn't start with, you know, Bob was brushing his teeth, he grabbed a thing of orange juice, he had breakfast, he kissed his wife goodbye, right? He, he was off to, he didn't, he didn't give that back information to start. You start in the middle of the action. And so pick the point of drama. And start there when you're going to open up a, a story. You can always go back and give background information. But remember, we've got really seconds to hook people. And so you want to create a reason to, to stick around a reason to hear the story, a reason to continue with the narrative. And so start in the middle. The next tip that I have for you is probably the most valuable tip that I can share with you. It's been the most valuable tip for me personally. And I first really, I first really learned this from, from Clayton. And that is, you want to avoid bald stories. And this is where I see, if honestly, they're, you know, besides a lack of drama in, in, in most stories, besides there being a lack of drama, like a lack of reason to care, to engage like a lack of, a reason to be intrigued by the storyline. The second biggest thing that I see done wrong, or done poorly with storytelling by marketers is they have what, what is referred to what Clayton referred to as the bald story. And a bald story is really nothing more than a story that is absent of enough detail to be able to see what is going on, like to be able to envision in your mind's eye what what's going on. And so the, the really the power of story, and this is where the value of, of, of, of reading fiction also comes into play, the right is in the detail is in the nuance, so that you can see it in your mind's eye. When a story is bald, or the balder, the more thinning a story is, the harder it is for you to envision it, and then therefore the less engaged you are with the story. And so it's the, it's the details, right. And so it's one thing to say like, you know, you know, Bob's big, you know, well, right, like Bob, you know, Bob, a big guy walked down the street, like, what do you mean a big guy? Was he tall? Was he, you know, overweight? Was he bouncer like? Was he a bodybuilder like? Right? Was he, you know, was he filled with, filled with, you know, tattoos, like, give some evidence, right? Like some, excuse me, some detail to the story. So, right, he didn't, you know, like, not just a car, right, but it could be a, you know, a beat up Silver Hyundai, right? Not just a pickup truck, but it could be, you know, a big black, you know, monster style pickup truck. It's the detail that allows you to envision it in your mind's eye. And oftentimes, it is the insignificant detail, or what seems insignificant, that makes it, that makes it exciting and valuable, right? And so, you know, my, the thing to, the thing, right, like is this goes back to specifics, you know, like concrete language, not just a gun, maybe he picked up, you know, he picked up a desert tan Glock, right? He picked up a desert tan Glock and, you know, and held it to the, you know, to the side gangster style. I don't know, right, but that, that brings you in. That conveys an image, right? Like, say, you know, like, I, you know, I drove home in, in my, in my car, and I grabbed a drink, and I put the TV on, right, is nowhere near as exciting, you know, like, as, right, I, I, I cruised up the highway, or I cruised down the highway, you know, whatever, I cruised down the highway in my convertible beamer thinking about work, went in, and I grabbed a glass of, you know, whatever, you know, bourbon, or whatnot, sat on the couch and popped on ESPN, right? It's real, it has a, I love it. I'm, also, I'll use the beard story, yeah, the beard story, right? And, and that's where that's like, that's story, that's good storytelling. And most poor storytelling, right? It's, it's bad, it's, it's poor storytelling, because there's no detail. Like, there's no detail. It's a very plain, flat image, right?
