And so, you know, exactly when, where, when, smells, sounds, sights, feeling, appeal to senses, it's all the stuff that we've talked about really before, carried over to story, the detail, right? The detail. There was a promotion that I did a couple of years ago for the, like the Agoura copywriting training that those guys did. And I told this story of how I went, of how I went to, you know, to, to Baltimore for the day, blah, blah, blah. And the, that, that promotion ended with me talking about how we went to grab dinner, right? Like, and we discussed copy secrets over dinner. And the whole thing was about copy secrets, right? And that's really why people were engaged to learn the, to learn the copywriting secrets, right? But what I did was I infused into the, the story, I infused little details, like so we ordered a glass of red wine, right? And, and so and so, sipped on, correct, that was for the Agoura, Agoura copy school, and so and so sipped on his wine, right? And he ordered a, you know, whatever, he ordered a, a, a cheeseburger deluxe, right? He took a second to put some ketchup on the fries and put, put a little salt on it. And then he dove in that, right? That detail added dimension to the story and it made it compelling. What seemed like insignificant detail was not insignificant detail at all. It's what made it intriguing and engaging. It created a picture. And so you want to avoid the bald story. That's the point. Next is show, don't tell. And this is really, right? Like, this is kind of writing 101, writing 101, if you will, and you could do a Google search for show, don't tell there are books, you know, show, don't tell. This is really all about, right? Again, what are we, I've said it before, we're the director of the movie playing in the mind of the prospect. And so don't just tell them, show them, let them envision. Let me give you a simple example of telling versus showing. John was mad, right? So what did we do? We told them what John was feeling, right? There's no picture painted. There's no action. There's no nothing demonstrating that, that John was mad. All we did was we were lazy and we said, John was mad. Well, a way to show that John was mad is John kicked the door open and slammed his briefcase on the table. I didn't have to come out and say John was mad. You get the feel that John was mad, right? Like you pick up from the action. I showed you that John was mad. And so, right? Like, you don't have to say I was, you know, I was cold, right? I was, you know, I was shivering. So I put my jacket on is an example of I'm telling you that I was cold, right? Like, I'm showing you that I was cold. I'm showing you through action, through movement, right? And so books on screenplays, absolutely. Save the Cat is a great book. I've recommended that before, many times before. Great book on screenwriting, on, you'll learn this idea of showing, don't telling. And so, simple, right? I don't want to be labor this point. This is stuff that you can, you can, you can Google if you want. The point is, right? I'm sensitive to this. I'm sensitive to when I'm giving away information versus when I'm painting a picture. Remember, I'm always trying to create images. I'm always trying to create images, right? Because like this, John kicked the door open and slammed the briefcase on the table, right? It stirs. Even if it's this little stirring of, right? Like, even if it's this little stirring, right? Like, of a feeling of emotion, whether it be like, stress, anxiety, anxiety, I want to see what's going on. I want to see why, like, versus John was mad, right? No dimension to that. Like, no intrigue to that. No picture to that. Next is, and this is what, this is, there, this is, if I believe, right, like, when it comes to content, and this, this includes storytelling. And this includes quotes in your copy and whatnot. This to me is, is one of the simplest yet, savviest things that you can do that helps ironically helps to bring down the FK score and makes the, the marketing more engaging. And that is include or incorporate dialogue into your stories. So, right? Like, don't just say, like, John told me about his mother or what, like, John told me about his mother or, you know, the professor, right? Like, I listened to the professor as he explained, XYZ, right? Instead, incorporate dialogue in there, right? Dialogue, like, share the dialogue, share what was said. If you look back, those of you that are familiar with that, that prior, that, that Agora promotion, that Agora promotion was almost all dialogue. Like, everything that I did was, it was almost all dialogue, right? I just use the dialogue to convey the beliefs and the, and the proof points and all the stuff that I wanted to convey. I did it through dialogue. And I did it through storytelling and dialogue. And it, it worked. It worked really well. It worked really well, I believe, because of the dialogue, right? Because the whole thing was dialogue and dialogue is a lot more engaging than just being told what was said, right? So, so sharing the dialogue, sharing the exchange, rather than just saying what was said. And so, and the key with dialogue, the key to, if you really want to take it up a level and you like, the, a person who I think is, is great at this. And the person that really made me start to pay attention to this was Mark Ford. And so Mark Ford is a guy that if you, you go, you can, I think his site is markford.net. He publishes, he still publishes like on a regular basis, just all kinds of weird stuff, recommendations, books, movies, essays, all on every topic that you can imagine. Mark is a true Renaissance man. And, and look at, look at Mark's essays. In, in 90% of his essays, you will find dialogue. There's some exchange, somebody said, right, and quotes it, it's in there, there's, there's a dialogue, there's an exchange. And whenever there's two characters talking, there's always a dialogue exchange. And the key with dialogue that makes it, that brings it up a level, right, is when you add what are called action beats. Action beats are when there is some movement. So no conversation is purely, do this, Todd said, right? Okay, I will, Brian said, right. The reason why you want to do this, Todd said, is to do, there's no justice back and forth in between there is right, like, do this, Todd said, here, here, go home, John said, as he glanced at his watch, right, you're going to be late for dinner. Right there, that, that is that little tidbit when you can incorporate that in there, right, like rather than say, I met up with a, I met up with this expert at a conference, and he told me that the single best thing to do is XYZ in my ads. Instead of doing that, which is simply telling, say, I met up, right, I met up with this expert at a, a conference, right, a big muscular dude, skull tattoo on the, on the left side of his, of his head, you know, an intimidating looking character. And here, you know, he looked at me with a weird scowl and then said, want to know the, want to know the secret to driving, you know, more, more, more sales on your webinars. Heck yeah, I responded with glee. Okay, let me tell you, he said, as he pulled up a chair for me to sit down. Boom. This is what you want to do. That is so much more engaging from a storytelling perspective than just simply saying, I met a dude at a conference, an expert guy, and here's what he told me. He said, I should go out and do XYZ on my blah, blah, blah, blah. That's garbage. That's garbage. And so these little tiny, you'll see little, right, little, little, little dialogue, little action beats can change the entire flavor of a, of a story. And so next, let's get into some tactics, some gambits, some, some little moves. I'm going to go through these really quickly. Many of you may already be familiar with this, but I want to share these in here because before we get into the language patterns, which to me is some of the, the, exactly. That's garbage, Todd, Todd said as he stroked his magnificent graying beard. What was I going to say? But the thing, the first thing that I want you to understand when we talk about tactics in general is this. And I think that this is a point for you to really kind of digest and, and, and really understand. And that is in marketing, right? A known tactic is a blown tactic. And so this has so many implications carried over. Like this has so many implications in marketing. My team has heard me say this a number of different times in a lot of different areas that when your prospect, look, let me say this and I want you to listen to me here. Okay. When, when your prospect knows what you're doing, when you are clearly being manipulative in your copy, when it screams of manipulation, your prospect knows and their resistance will, will come up. In fact, many people, even if they are interested, will not buy because they know you are clearly trying to manipulate them. And so you've got to recognize that, right? You are better off being subtle with what you do, right? Then being, uh, than being overzealous and having it be apparent to your prospect, what it is that you're trying to do. And so you're better off subtle. It subtle is much more powerful than is this overzealous approach, which is it's, it's screaming manipulation. It's screaming like that you are, are, are trying to convince them. It's screaming that you are trying to push them to do, to do something. It's screaming that you are trying to, uh, to get them to think a certain way or feel a certain way, right? Subtlety is enormously powerful in copy. Subtlety is enormously powerful in, um, in marketing strategy and tactical execution. When a prospect knows what it is that you are doing, a known tactic is a blown tactic. What that means is that a known tactic, a recognized tactic is an ineffective tactic, meaning it will not work, right? It will not work. Now there are, of course, like, like, look, you know, there are, you know, you know, you, we certainly could, could get into, um, you know, the kind of semantics of like, you know, like some of you said, um, that you invested in this training for fear of missing out, right? You didn't want to miss out. And, you know, that was very real and right, like, I'm not going to do this again. I have no plans on doing this again. I have no plans on releasing these recordings anytime soon. Um, right. I told you guys the purpose and aim of this. Um, and so there was a very legitimate real fear. There are a lot of people that missed out on this, right? We saw, uh, and you may have known that, like, you may have known that that, that is a tactic or that is a tactic that people use to create this fear of missing out. Um, but that doesn't mean that in this context, because of our relationship, you knew that it was real, right? Because of our relationship, you knew that, right? When I was saying that I'm not, I'm not, you know, like when the countdown timer hits, it's gone, it's gone, right? And that I'm not doing this again. You knew me, you know me, you know my reputation. And so you knew that that was real. Um, and, and, and so there, there are certainly, there are certainly nuances just because you recognize you were smart enough to know what was at work there doesn't mean that, um, doesn't mean that in certain instances that it's not going to have, um, the, the, the impact. But for the general market, the, on the front end, right, you've got to recognize that a known tactic is a blown tactic, right? A known tactic is a blown tactic. And as a side note, this is why, and I've shared this story many times before, this is why, um, many moons ago when Rich Sheffrin and I were discussing the difference between the really savvy marketers and the average marketers, we talked about the difference isn't in the strategies that they use, right? So the savvy marketers will still use things like, uh, you know, commitment and consistency and authority and reciprocity and scarcity and urgency, uh, you know, social proof and, and all that. But the savvy marketer will look for the new and different tactical ways to execute those same strategies, right? Like new, what's a new and different way to demonstrate social proof? What's a new and different way to, to demonstrate or trigger social proof, right? And so the strategy is the same, but the tactical way of executing it is really the difference between, among other things, is really the difference between the, um, the, uh, the, the, the, the all star marketer and the, the average marketer. You just have to recognize this. This is the thing that I want you to kind of absorb and ponder that if you are too explicit in what it is that you're trying to do, and it's crystal clear to your prospect that you are trying to manipulate them. You are dead in the water, right? You're dead in the water. And that's why I'm always very sensitive to when I say certain things. And that's, that's why I like the language patterns. And I love certain things like, um, presuppositional statements and presuppositional questions because they are kind of much more, they're subtle, more, um, more nuanced ways of communicating certain things that, that if we just came out and said it, um, it would trigger that like, right? There are things like, for example, let me say this, there are things like, um, uh, what was I going to say, you know, when, when we tap into like, you know, oftentimes marketers talk about, you know, like, why does somebody buy a, uh, a, you know, a Rolex, right? Like, why does somebody buy a Rolex, right? Somebody buys a Rolex, not because it keeps better time than a Seiko or something like that, but you buy a Rolex, right? Sometimes it's because they can, right? Oftentimes it's, there's something deeper, right? That's the justification that they tell themselves, right? Like, I know when I've made, uh, expensive purchases, right? I tell myself, right, I deserve it, or I can, right? But there's really something more going on underneath. Let's be honest about that. Right? Might be status, right? I want to demonstrate. I don't want, you know, like, uh, you know, like part of it is, yeah, I want people to know I'm, I'm the man, you know, kind of thing, kind of attitude. Um, I think that I've, uh, grown out of that, uh, I would like to think that to some extent I've grown out of it. That could be also me telling myself that, um, could be insecurity, right? But here, here is the thing, right? So let's take something like, um, you know, um, status, or let's take something like insecurity, right? So what you're not going to do is you're not going to come out and we're not going to talk about, like, get this Rolex so that, you know, like, uh, you'll no longer feel insecure, right? You'll now feel like, you know, you'll feel like, um, one of the crowd, you will, right? You'll finally feel like you've, uh, you know, finally feel successful, no longer feel insecure. Um, finally own this timepiece and make sure everybody knows how successful you are, right? Like you're not going to do that, right? You're not going to do that in your copy. Why are you not going to do that? Because you're going to trigger to the prospect, like, ooh, that feels just icky and ugly and like that's manipulative and right? Like I know what you're trying to do, right? It's apparent. Now look, we might say that, um, part of our, our, our, our thing might be, right? Like, um, uh, you know, um, people comment to you, um, you know, you know, people start to comment, um, what a nice timepiece you've got, right? Or you notice, right? You notice how your friends start to, like you can see out of the corner of your eye, your, your, your friends start to glance down at your wrist, right? That is a much more subtle way to convey, right? Where it's being done implicitly rather than explicitly, right? Like the, the, the prospect is picking up on what's being said. It's the undertone. It's the underlying text, so to speak, that is being communicated. And so you've just got to recognize that there are some things, right? That like, if you are too aggressive and too explicit in, you're going to trigger that. I know exactly what, what they're doing. I could like, I know, man, like, whoa, easy, right? Tone it down, like, right? And so you've just got to recognize this carries over in so many different areas of, of, uh, of, of marketing. With that being said, let's get into some of the kind of the go-to, the go-to, uh, you know, tactics, gambits, moves, if you will. Like I said, some of these, you're going to be, um, you're going to be familiar with. I just want to give you my nuances. So the, the takeaway, the reality is, is that the takeaway is, is all about, right? Like at the end of the day, here, I'll tell you, I'll give you this mindset that, um, you, you never want your, the takeaway, there's this very specific kind of gambit play here, but the premise, the underlying philosophy that I want you to have with this is you never want to appear needy in your copy. You never want to appear that you are pushing the sale. What you want to appear is that you are leading them to a conclusion and then giving them the opportunity to buy, right? And the attitude that you want to maintain is like, and I love the way that Zig Ziglar, uh, many, many moons ago said it when he was like, like, whether you buy or not, I'm having filet mignon tonight. Like meaning like the, not this, not this idea of, um, I don't mean this idea of cockiness, right? Like I, I'm not going to have filet mignon tonight. Um, right? This idea of like, um, yeah, like I would say an AD and that's a great way to say it. Like the, like, I'm not trying to force you. I'm not trying to, like, I'm not coming on aggressive and trying to push you to make the thing. I'm not, I'm not trying to be overzealous and like this thing is, is like, you need this. You, you absolutely need this right now. You want this without this. You're going to, like, I don't want to ever appear pushy. I want to appear in the opposite. Like, look, you know, let me tell you what, what, what this thing, why I think that you should be excited about this thing. Why I believe that this thing is a, um, for you will be a game changer and, and here it is. But this is also not for you, right? If you blah, blah, blah, blah, this is not for you. If you're not interested, like, if you're not serious, if you're not, um, you know, into XYZ, if you're just looking for a fast thing, if you're a fly by night or like, or this isn't for you. If you're like a, a mean person with a scarcity mindset, like, I have no interest in working with, with people like that. And so I'd rather not take your money, right? Like, that makes things more attractive. It makes things more attractive, not only because of the whole idea, the whole principle that people want things that they're, they're, they're, they're pulled away from, right? People don't, you know, people want like they, you know, we want what we can't have, right? It's a weird, like, once you get it, then you're like, right? Like, it's the, the, when you were younger and you chased the, the girl or the guy and you were like desperate and it was top of mind. And then you got him and you were like, ah, man, this is, this is not this, you know, uh, this is all, right? And so, right? Like, but, uh, right? It, it, um, it's also like JMC said in there, it is, um, it's not what they expect. And so in and of itself, while it seems aggressive, like, this might not be right for you, right? Like this, this may not be right for you. This is, this is actual something that I said in the copy for this workshop. This, this, this is not for everybody. This may not be right for you. This is not for XYZ, LMNOP. This is only for the serious marketer who wants serious copy chops, right? And you can tell, right? The reality is, right? Like, that came from a very real place. That came from a place of authenticity, right? Cause I know that the person who just wants push button, the guy or the guy who's looking for some AI piece of software, ain't interested in pluralizing the verb and active voice and passive voice and, and tense and, and all that. And so the reality is, right? That while it's, while it served as a marketing, uh, a marketing tactic and it was done intentionally, it was also out of this authentic belief that it's not for everybody. It absolutely does. And it's also more believable. Like Lynn said, spot on. You guys are just on the freaking money, right? And so there is this, right? This is not for you. This may not be right for you. You should pass on this offer unless you, another way of doing this is like, you know, if you qualify, if you meet the following criteria, right? Some of that is a little bit more, you know, is a little bit stronger. Like, you know, like if you qualify is, you know, we see that all the time, the whole velvet rope thing, right? Blah, blah, blah, blah. But the principle behind this, right? Is it really the principle behind this for me is I, if anything, to just say it plainly, if anything I want to come across as I really don't care whether you buy or you don't, right? I'm like, look, this is what I found. Here is why I found this to be the thing. Here is an amazing offer like that I put together. What I believe is an amazing offer. If you think so as well, cool, jump at it. If not, then the same for you. And that's cool too, right? That's the attitude that I, you know, that I take with all the copy. And I think that that is really critical in the vibe, right? The vibe is always, I'm not pushing, I'm leading, right? I'm not shoving, right? I'm saying like, come here, let me show you something. Come here, let me show you something else. What do you think about this? And check this out. And right, like, and hey, my, like, hey, if you see what I see and you see what everybody else that I showed this to sees, then let me tell you about a cool opportunity that you may want to jump on right now, right? And here it is. That's the attitude. And the takeaway is part of how I'm able to convey that, right? And so I don't want, no, nobody wants to be pushed. Nobody wants to be pushed, right? They want to be led. In fact, they want to lead. They want to be given the opportunity to lead. And so the takeaway is part of how we, how we do that. And this is a way of disarming, because like JMC said as well, it's not what they expect, right? They don't expect you to want to try to talk them out of it. They expect you to, they expect you to try to push it on them, right? They expect you like, oh, he's going to tell me why this is great. He's going to tell me why I should buy right now. And he's going to tell me why this is perfect for me. And he's going to tell me why this is going to change my whole life and why this meets every criteria. Like when people ask me, is this right for me? Like, Todd, is this right for me? And what they expect me to say, right, is always absolutely, of course it is. Of course it is, right? Like, of course it is. And my answer is, I don't know, right? I'm not sure if this is right for you. Maybe it may not be, right? And part of that, let me tell you, part of that is, right, that thankfully, thankfully, you know, I'm blessed to be in that position, right? As well, it's what I believe. And so I'm able to live out my belief in, I don't want to take somebody's money if it's not right for them. But it's also a very effective marketing tactic, right? And so there is this mix at play. And there's always that mix at play. So I will never use a tactic that doesn't come from a place of authenticity, never ever, right? Never ever will I use a tactic that doesn't come from a place of authenticity. And so next thing that I want to talk about, which is also disarming, is the damaging admission, which some of you also may be familiar with. And when done correctly, this is also extremely disarming because it, number one, it's not what the prospect expects. And also it can serve when done correctly, like I'm going to show you as a, as a benefit. So the damaging admission is when you admit, and this is important, this is how I, this is how I've, how I use the damaging admission. So others may describe this a little bit differently or kind of articulate this a little bit differently or even use this a little bit differently. But this is how I do it. So, so Scott said, if you can point out your flaws before they find them, they stop looking. So that's, that is definitely one way to use the damaging admission, right? And so I don't use it that way, but that is one way to use it. And that's not, I'm not saying that that's the wrong way to use it. That's just another way to use it. I use it in a very specific context. So I'm using the damaging admission for a very specific reason. And that very specific reason is to build credibility and disarm the, the, the prospect. So build credibility and disarm the prospect. So in this context, for me, when I'm, when I'm doing that, I am admitting a negative about the product solution or mechanism, but the word in there that's important is insignificant, meaning I am pointing something out that is a negative that I know is not a, or make or break to the sale, meaning I'm admitting a negative that I know the prospect doesn't really care about anyway, right? And so by me admitting the negative about something that they don't care about, what it does, they don't care about the fact, they don't care about the negative. What they do care about is that I just admitted a negative, which makes everything else that I've said that much more credible because anybody, any marketer, any salesperson, any entrepreneur that would admit a negative, well, maybe likely everything else that he said then is, he seems honest because he's willing to admit a negative. Most people hide all the negatives, don't say anything negative, everything is positive, this thing is perfect, greatest, flawless, unbelievable. And so it makes everything else that I've said credible and that much more believable. And it makes them feel like, all right, this dude's legit, real honest. And so I am admitting a negative, but I am strategically choosing the negative that I am admitting to make sure that I'm focusing on something insignificant to them. So a great example is in my very first campaign in my very first sales letter that I wrote almost two decades ago, I was selling a home study kit for massage therapists on how to grow their practice. So this was the first sales letter that I wrote for my own business. So I was helping massage therapists to grow their practice with marketing, salesmanship, blah, blah, blah. And so I was selling this home study kit, show you how long ago this was, it came with a file, excuse me, a diskette, like a hard diskette, some of you don't even know what that is, a hard diskette with like templates and swipe files on it. It came with cassette tapes, cassette tapes and binders, three ring binders of stuff. It was this monster like $350 home study thing, I modeled it after the first information product that I purchased. And in the sales letter, right, so this thing was, it was all black and white, like with the exception of the covers in the, the cover in the binders were yellow, like yellow paper with black, black writing on it, black letters on it. And then inside it was just all black and white. So there was no graphics, no four colors, no fancy covers, no fancy images, this whole thing was like, it was disgusting, it was ugly. And so in the copy, I said something along the lines of, and just a heads up, right, you won't find any fancy graphics, any four color, you know, images, or any, any fancy covers. This thing is, I said something like this, you know, this is not the kind of thing that you're going to want to display proudly on your, on your bookshelf. If that's what you're looking for, this is not for you. But this is stuff, this is, you know, it is jam packed with, you know, blah, blah, blah techniques. And so if that's what you're interested in, you're going to love this. So it was this combination of, right, this damaging admission, and a takeaway, like if that's what you're looking for, this isn't for you, blah, blah, blah, almost like it was this takeaway was also like the identity, like if that's you, if you're just looking to display something nice on your bookshelf, then right, like, if that's who you are, then this isn't for you, I'm kind of thing. But I knew that that was insignificant, because they weren't buying the home study course for the fancy pants, graphics, or images, they didn't care about that. And so for me, right, that became this damaging admission, damaging admission, but it wasn't really damaging, right, because it didn't damage the sale, and it just added credibility. I just admitted something negative, right. And so why would he admit something negative unless everything else that he said was true? And so that's a simple way of using the damaging, the damaging admission. And so I always look for an insignificant negative, like an insignificant negative, right, like some, and sometimes I look for, and this is the savvier play, I look for, how can I make a damaging admission a positive? Or how can I almost, how can I admit something, right, and make it a positive? Or how can I admit something that to the prospect, right, is a positive? So let me give you an example of both of these. So turning a damaging admission into a positive is something like this, let's say I was selling a piece of software for something, I might say, like, look, let me be clear with you, this thing, right, it only does one thing, right, it only does this one thing, it doesn't do 12 different things, and that's because we wanted to focus on making it the best at that. That's a, that's a seeming, right, like if I was talking about, let's say, you know, if I was selling a piece of software, graphic design software, and let's say hypothetically, I'm just using this as an illustration, it all, it was focused on removing the background of pictures, so you could upload an image and it would automatically remove the background. I know there are free versions of this, just roll with me, right, and so that's what it did, right, I might say, like, let me be clear, this thing only does one thing, it removes, it removes backgrounds, it doesn't do text, it doesn't do filters, it doesn't do this, it doesn't do any of that other stuff, and that's because we wanted to focus on making this the absolute cleanest, clearest, purest background remover on the planet. Now, if that was the hook that got them into the campaign, and that's what they were interested in, well, then that damaging admission is not really damaging at all, it really bolsters the value of what it is that we did, because the thing that they care about is the thing that we said we just wanted to focus on that. If you're looking for like an all-in-one, you know, like, you know, an all-in-one thing, this isn't, this isn't for you kind of thing, and so that is an example of the damaging admission turned into a positive. Now, I could actually do a damaging admission that is almost, it seems like it's, I'm admitting something, but to the prospect, they're like, that's awesome, right, so let's say I said something like, you know, you know, like they were signing up for a coaching program, right, or something like that, and I said something like, look, heads up, after you enroll, you won't be able to get started right away, you'll probably have to wait about 10 to 15 hours before your personal concierge reaches out to you, just want you to be aware of that, like something like that, right, well, they might say, dang, I didn't even know that I was going to get a call that quickly, right, and so I'm saying it as if it's a negative, like, look, a little heads up, you're not going to be able to get started instantly, you're going to have to wait probably like 10, 15 hours before you get your first call from your personal concierge to get rolling, as long as you're cool with that, then you can get started now, something like that, that is what, right, I'm admitting something, the credibility, right, but it's not really a negative at all, to the prospect, they might be saying, dang, that's awesome, I didn't even think I was going to get a call that quick, I thought I was going to have to wait, and so that's the damaging admission. gives it away, and it also, right, it shows the cards, and so, like, again, right, I want the, I want the impact, I'd rather have less impact, right, than no, like, than try to do something big enough, no impact, and so I would take out mediocre, and so, um, next is, you guys know this, open loops, nested loops, open loops, or little cliff hangers, open loops, you guys know, I'm sure you guys are all familiar with open loops, open loops, when we bring up an idea, topic, something that they're interested in, and then we say more about that in a minute, more about that in just a minute, I'll get to that in just a bit, or more about that in a second, right, or in a moment, I'm going to tell you x, y, z, in a moment, I'm going to tell you the single most valuable secret to, to saving 80% on your taxes. First, let me tell you about, uh, why this is, so why this is working right now, that is what's called an open loop, right, it opens a, it opens a gap in the prospects mind that they now want filled, this is why you hear on news programs, right, uh, Kanye West, you know, caught with, um, an illegal substance, more about that, you know, more about that coming up in our next segment, first, let's go to the weather, you know, and now all of a sudden you're like, what the heck was the illegal substance is bad, like, or, right, like, and so it is this, um, it is this, uh, this open gap, the, the, what you, the, the savviest way to apply this, and, and I want to be careful in terms of time here, the savviest way to apply this is nested, and nested means that you don't close one loop until you open another loop, and so there's always a gap that the prospect, right, like you never bring the prospect until the end of the promotion to this conclusion, like, right, there's no more for me to know, right, so there should always be this, this, um, this, this open loop, right, so if we said something like, um, you know, like, um, in a moment, I'm going to tell you the single, you know, the single most effective way to set, uh, to save 50% of your taxes, first, let me tell you about, um, you know, what is being done for you, uh, what is being done to you, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, um, um, you know, um, fortunately there's a way to capitalize on these change, these changes in the, um, in, in, in the laws, right, let me first tell you how to save that 50% on your taxes. Now we opened one, right, and now we're going to, so we opened one, before we close that, we opened another, then we close the first one, then we open another, then we close the second one, then we open another, then we close the third one, and so there's always this gap left open, right, they can never, right, in other words, what we're doing is we are always keeping the line tight, never giving them the opportunity to swim off because there's always one more piece to the puzzle that's left out there, and so it's those, those gaps, those constant opening of a gap, closing a gap, opening of a gap, closing of a gap, opening of a gap, closing of a gap, right, like, I'll tell you, you know, I'll tell you why shortly. Let me explain how to save 50% on your taxes, right, and then we go, da, da, da, da, da, and then, right, we're going to go, and we're going to constantly open these loops, that's how you keep that tension there, you, you, you, it's almost this, you know, we don't, we're creating this, um, you know, this, for lack of a better phrase, mental distress, that, um, where they need closure, it's the need for closure on this information that they care about, this gap, this curiosity gap that they need, right, and it's that tension that keeps them from bailing, like, I don't want to leave now because I'm gonna miss out on that, and then, right, and so before, if we close that, and then there's nothing else, now they can leave and that mental tension is relieved, that, right, their, their tension relief, stop, done, I'm out, right, I'm out, it's almost like this, if you've ever watched a, um, you watch, how many of you used to watch, like, Lost, the show Lost, how many of you watched the, it is the, the, the Zegernick effect, how many of you watched the show Lost, who did, say me if you did. JMC, Ted, Marco, cool, so yeah, that show was great, right, neat, right, so number one, right, the show, okay, so a lot of you, a handful of you, so, right, so that show, right, like, and, like, many good shows, like the show 24, right, like, there was a cliffhanger always, right, like, there was a cliffhanger always, um, and right, like, half the time at the end, right, with, with Lost, I was was, what's going on, I need to know what was going on, and the thing was right, that what they did was, was right, like, they, um, and this is my tendency, like, listen to me, so when I watch shows, like, if I, if I watch a show, what happens is, right, so watch a show, I'll watch a first episode, and then cliffhanger in the end, and I'm like, yeah, the hatch, the numbers, what the heck is going on, the smoke in the woods or whatever, uh, and I watch a show and it comes to a cliffhanger, and now I'm like, I need, I gotta know what the, like, if I'm interested, I gotta know, I gotta know what's going on here, right, and so I watch the next episode, right, and, but what happens is, for me, is if I don't finish, let's say, the next episode, so cliffhanger, sometimes in the smart shows, there's no more gap, there's no more mental distress for me, like, there's nothing left open that I need to know, and so that's why sometimes, like, what I do when I, when I want to stop, like, if I'm, if I find myself, like, when I'm decompressing and I'm like, I want to binge a show, right, I want to binge a show, what I do is, when I'm watching an episode, I always stop that episode 15 minutes before the end, right, 15 minutes before the end, why, because I don't want to get exposed to that cliffhanger because I don't want the mental distress of needing to know the resolution to that cliffhanger, excuse me, and so I'll stop it 15 minutes before the end, because I'm like, all right, I could sleep peacefully, like, there's no unresolved issues here going on, and so I'm done, right, but that shows you the power of nested loops, because it's that tension, right, that prevents the checking out of the prospect, right, like, it's almost like not only do they want to know, not only are we presenting information that they care about, but there is a level and aspect of mental distress, even that is like on an unconscious level, this need for closure, right, like, I need to close this, like, right, and, and the funny thing too is the, the further in you get, the further in you get, the more of that mental distress, that need for closure there is. So let's say I said to you, there are five ways, five tactics to do x, y, z, and, right, and one, two, three, and I got you all the way to four, right, well, bailing at four without getting the fifth one, there is more mental distress, more need for closure, that if you only got one, and we didn't get to two, three, four, and five, right, and so the further along the long, when you can, when you can use these nested loops, the deeper in that you can get people, right, the more likely they are to complete it, this is why, understand, this is why, and I want you to ponder this, this is why the deeper in your copy, you can get a prospect, the more likely they are to complete it, right, the more likely they are to finish, so I'm not saying that they will finish, right, the more likely they are to finish, the deeper in you go, the more likely they are to finish, and so that's why, right, when we use nested loops, right, we're creating that tension, we're not letting any slack in there, we're not giving them an opportunity to bail, right, we're keeping that mental, mental distress or that really, that need for closure, right, and the deeper we can bring them, the more likely they are to complete it, and that operates on so many, so many different levels, so many different levels, by the way, as a side note, right, that's why, you know, like, that's partially why the deeper in, that's partially why like, car dealerships, you know, in most car dealerships, you go in, right, they keep you there for three hours, right, because the likelihood of you exit, the likelihood of you being like, ah, I'm not going ahead with this, is a lot less when you're three hours in than when you're 15 minutes in, right, like, you know what I mean, like, and so it's the whole idea of sunk cost, and so we already did this, we already did this, we already did this, okay, and we already did this, so now let's get to, let's go to our final section, and that is language patterns, so this is going to be fun, this is going to be fun, because these are little bits of copy, we'll be able to, I believe, cruise through this, there's a lot here, but we'll be able to cruise through this quickly, so like I said, probably maybe 530-ish, no and me will be here till eight, no, we should be done around 530-ish, and so this is, we're going to cruise through this quickly, because I'm going to give you examples of these different patterns, I'll explain to you what they're used for, where you can use them, and you can pick and choose these, right, so this is kind of like a, here's a whole bunch of goodies that you can use, that you can kind of sprinkle throughout your copy, and you can pick and choose these depending on the impact that you are looking for, so the first language pattern is a presuppositional statement, now for those of you that are not familiar with presuppositions, I love this, I've talked about this for years now, and I use presuppositions all the time, and so a presupposition is, well in this context, a presuppositional statement is a statement that presupposes a truth, meaning buried in the statement itself, there is a pre-established belief, so there is a belief that is almost taken for granted in the statement itself, you're going to see how, meaning there's something that has to be believed already to believe this statement, and so basically this is a way to get your prospect to think a certain way without them realizing really what's being done, to be honest with you, and so this is an example of this, this is the format, this is the the darkened bullets, I forget who I first learned this from, or where I learned this, it was a collection from, I can't remember where I learned this from, but you could probably Google presuppositional, and so David, so I'm not a fan of the typical NLP stuff, I don't believe in the whole embedded commands kind of thing, but I believe in the power of language and structure, and from studying argumentation and understanding debate and argumentation and how to prove a point, I see how presuppositions are used to manipulate perspective, and I use manipulate not in the harsh term, but so if describing the activity that your prospect likely does, then what's the action that you want them to take? So if you write copy for your business, then using presuppositional statements is a smart move for you, and so we're tying the action that I want, like if I was selling them, if I was selling folks on using presuppositional statements, like if I was talking about the big marketing idea, like if you run paid ads, if you run paid ads and are concerned about compliance, then using big marketing ideas in your campaigns is a smart move for you, see that's a language pattern, if you're doing X, then using L-O-M-N-O-P is smart, or doing XYZ is smart, and it is a form of, if this then that, if you're doing this, then this is good for you, and here is, if you are, here let me give you the example, because this is here just for the slides really for you, same thing, if you're creating marketing promotions, there's an excellent chance that you're interested in increasing your sales conversions, so see how this is a softer way of, it's a softer way, a more subtle way of talking about, I love it, David, keep those coming, if you're X then do Y, keep them coming, I've never studied NLP, and so I'd love to hear your, sprinkle your add-on in here, if there's some NLP goodness that you can add to this, and so a subtle way, it's softer, it doesn't have that aggressive, the typical copywriter writes, if you're creating marketing promotions then you need to, or you should focus on increasing your sales, you need to increase your sales, see how that feels manipulative, that feels more aggressive, that feels like you are, you're overstepping, whereas if you're creating marketing promotions there's an excellent chance that you're interested in increasing your sales conversions, is a softer way of doing it, there's another common, every time you do something which doesn't lead to you, every time you launch a new Facebook ad without a big marketing idea you risk your ad account getting flagged, here are all little language patterns, every successful golfer knows the necessity of mastering the short game, this is a beautiful one, you see I use this often and you likely see this used often, I actually, this was used in the promotion for this workshop, the beauty is that, this is that whole, we talked a little bit earlier about claim, fact, repositioning framing, claims as fact, here's another way to do it, I could say that every serious marketer, every successful marketer knows the necessity of a high AOV, so if I wanted the prospect to, if I was marketing and selling the idea of why you need a high AOV and I said every successful marketer knows the necessity today in 2021 of having a high AOV, what am I doing, I am presenting a claim as an established, assumed known fact by the marketers, the people that they want to identify with, so this is leveraging identity, it's leveraging authority and it's taking a claim and presenting it as fact, and so presenting it as fact, this is a simple, all the best marketers know, all the best marketers use, the smartest marketers focus on, all of that is this subtle shift, that little language pattern, that subtle shift in, well if they know it and do it and I want to be like them, maybe I should know it and do it, right, like little subtle shift, next is, and this is my favorite, I use this in a certain format which I'll show you in just a second, presuppositional questions, so this is an example like what, so this is great, I use this almost all the time, this is what really set me off years ago on understanding the different ways to use presuppositions through marketing, so check out this question here, this might be a question that I pose in marketing in a campaign in a piece of copy, I might say, maybe so now, maybe you're wondering what makes the E5 method so effective at generating new sales every day, you may be wondering, so what makes the E5 method so effective at generating new sales every day, so what is it about the E5 method that makes it so effective at generating new sales every day, see the power of that question is that there is a presupposition in there and that presupposition right is that the E5 method is so effective at generating new sales, but because the question is focused on what makes the E5 method so effective at generating new sales every day, the in order for the prospect to even think of the answer, they have to accept that the E5 method is so effective at generating new sales every day, see in other words, I'm posing a question to them based on this presupposition that this thing is insanely powerful and getting them to say, yeah, what does make it so effective at generating new sales every day, meaning that the thing that I care about, the thing that I want them to believe is that the E5 method is so effective at generating new sales every day, I don't really care about the why, in other words, really that's not what, right, that is not what the, in the copy, I just say, maybe you're wondering, why is this thing so freaking powerful at bringing in new buyers every single day, what is it about this thing and they go, yeah, what is it about this thing, man, that makes it so powerful, like, help me to understand why is it so powerful, meanwhile, what they're accepting because of the presupposition is that this thing is so freaking powerful, right, and so same thing with this, so how quickly will you result above here, so how quickly will you see, how quickly will you see daily new sales when using the E5 method, you're wondering, maybe you maybe wonder, I might just say, right, so maybe you're wondering, like, how quickly will you see daily new sales when using the E5 method, now check this out, another insanely powerful question, insanely powerful question, insanely valuable question, why, because there's a presupposition buried in there, what is the presupposition, that you will see daily new sales when using the E5 method, the question is not about whether you will or you won't, the question is about how quickly you will, and so in order for them to say, yeah, how quickly will I start seeing new buyers every day with this thing, like, like, right, that's the, that's the, the, the, the, how quickly will I see daily new buyers with this, and right, all I care about is that they're, they're processing, they're thinking, they're, they're, they're, they have to accept the presupposition to even, to even ponder the answer to the question, they have to accept the, they have to accept the presupposition to even ponder the question, remember the question isn't about will you see daily new sales, it's how quickly, right, like, how quickly will you see, you know, daily, daily new sales, or if I said something like, so what is it about the E5 method that, that allows it to go to work so quickly, generating new buyers for you, like, maybe you're wondering what is it about the way the E5 method works to go to work, you know, to quickly produce so many new buyers for you, that's a presupposition buried in there, right, that they can't even ponder the question, right, without accepting that presupposition, and it's the presupposition that I want them to accept, that's the thing that I, right, that it's powerful, that it works, that it works, that they'll get new buyers, what, you know, that it is effective at generating new buyers, that, and you'll see this, you'll see me use this on a regular basis, on a regular basis, and so, oh, I love that, let me, I gotta, I gotta, let me see here, I gotta make sure, there you go, I gotta bring this bad boy up, thank you, see, now that's something that I've never seen, and so thank you for that, both of you guys, and so here's another, like, here are more presuppositions, and this is a way, right, like, here, how traumatic was your last argument with your partner, right, like, this is me, right, if I was selling a relationship building thing, right, like, you know, relationship building, core relationship repair thing, how traumatic was your last argument with your partner, you'll notice there's a presupposition in there, right, like, I didn't ask was your last argument traumatic, I said how traumatic, right, how traumatic was your last argument, the presupposition is that your last argument was traumatic, there's also a presupposition in there that you had an argument, right, and so, right, like, and so you see how these questions, right, like, you see how questions posed, right, throughout copy, first of all, become this back and forth, right, like, this get them thinking, get them reengaged, and also this gets them thinking in the direction that you, you, you, you want, right, how damaging is it to your relationship when you argue with your partner, how damaging do you think, how damaging is it to your relationship when you argue with your partner, right, like, see the presupposition in there is that it's damaging to your relationship, I didn't say is it, I said how damaging, so this presupposes that it is damaging, I just need you to tell me how damaging it is to your, to your, to your relationship, there's another type of language pattern that you can use, right, like, you know, like, you know, almost like how ineffective, or how much money do you lose when you run Facebook ads, right, is, is a presuppositional question because it presupposes that you're losing money when it comes to your, your, your Facebook ads, right, it presupposes that, and so it forces the prospect's mind to go in that direction. And so I would have stated the guarantee very differently. I would rather have had a guarantee that was either, that was more audacious, but didn't negate what it is that I just said, right. And so I would rather have had a guarantee that was like, like you're either thrilled and blown away by how resistant this thing is to, to anything and everything you do to it or return it in the next 30 days for all your money back plus double or whatever, right. And so rather than give a guarantee that completely negates, right, that negates what it is that they just said. And so, right, so you flip the guarantee rather than if you're dissatisfied, which is focusing on the negative, right. If you're dissatisfied, if you are unhappy, right. If you don't, like, like when somebody says, you will get, you'll get, let's say in high ticket coaching, you'll get a dozen clients a month, right. You'll get a dozen clients a month and then they get to the guarantee and they say, right, like, if you don't get at least a dozen clients a month, we'll return your money. You just said and gave me this whole argument that like, I'm definitely going to get this 12 clients a month. And now you're saying if I don't get these 12 clients a month. And so it takes away from the confidence in the claim. And so all it is is a slight change in language. And so we flip it from the negative to the positive. Instead of saying if you're dissatisfied, you just say you're either head over heels thrilled with the results, right, or return the widget, right. And so like I would say, right, take the, you know, you either get 12, you know, you get 12, you're going to get 12 clients a month. And if you don't get at least 12 clients a month, we're going to give you your money back, right. I would probably say you're either blown away by the volume of clients that we get you, you're thrilled with the amount of clients that you are getting every day, thanks to this system. You know, a minimum of 12 or send one email and you'll get all of your money back, right. Like, so I'm going to flip it to the positive. I'm not going to say if you're unhappy, I'm going to say you're either happy or so I'm going to keep it focused on the positive. So if you remember when we talked yesterday about the, when we talked about, and somebody had asked about, about like the dimensionalized benefit, and I talked about this idea that, that you have to be careful, you want to be specific, but you want to be universal enough to apply, apply to everybody. So you want to dimensionalize and you want to paint the picture, but you've got to be universal enough to apply for everybody. You don't want to make the mistake of being too specific in the picture that you paint where other people are like, that's not me, that doesn't apply to me. you know, some folks believe that or feel that, and I think that there's value in both of these, but I lean towards the don't miss out, right, because the fear of missing out today is so powerful, stronger driver than is the positive, and so I tend to go with if you don't want to miss out, and you could see that, for those of you that paid attention, like, even in the, in the, in the, in the, Another language pattern, most million dollar real estate agents agree the ability to generate consistent sales is lifeblood of this business, same type of authority, right, like we talked about already just another language pattern to do this, most million dollar real estate agents agree, right, that the ability to generate, right, that is taking a claim and giving it a level of credibility and authority that wouldn't have, if we took that away, if all we said is the ability to generate consistent sales is the lifeblood of this business, that's just a pure claim, right, but when we say most million dollar real estate agents agree, the ability to generate consistent clients is the lifeblood of this business adds that authority to the statement, right, and so it changes the framing of the statement as the best, most successful, here, another one as the most successful dentist say, practice growth comes from patient retention, it's not just another way to do the, to do the same thing.
